root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Modeling Reference Guide
root.summary: Front matter and the main Cúram 8.1.2 modeling reference. It introduces the UML input meta-model and generator flow, then covers packages, stereotypes, data types, audit mappings, domain definitions and overrides, entity/facade/process/struct classes, attributes, operations, mandatory parameter fields, and entity CRUD/batch/handcrafted SQL behaviors. It also includes aggregation, assign(), foreign keys, indexes/unique indexes, generated class hierarchy and factories, JMS queue connectors/field mappers, subclass modeling, and application customization guidance. Useful as the top-level entry point for the full modeling guide.

children.title: Note
children.summary: This note directs readers to review the “Notices” section on page 113 before using the product or the information in the document. It also includes the copyright notice for Merative US L.P. covering 2012 and 2024.

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Edition and front-matter for the Cúram 8.1/8.1.1/8.1.2 documentation, followed by the contents and the start of the Cúram modeling reference. It introduces the Cúram UML-based generator, the input meta-model, architecture layers, and the main model stereotypes used by the generator (class, attribute, and operation stereotypes). The contents also show the full reference structure for entities, processes, structs, operations, JMS queue connectors, subclass modeling, and application customization.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: Pages 15–24 cover Cúram modeling reference topics: operation stereotypes for data access and JMS/deferred processing, relationship stereotypes, and the full set of Cúram data types. They explain package behavior and the CODE_PACKAGE option, including naming/override rules and generated class locations. The section also details AuditMappings classes, their implementation requirements, audit-related operation stereotypes, and insert/modify exclusion options. Finally, it introduces domain definition classes and options such as code table names, code table roots, whitespace handling, uppercase conversion, and custom validation functions.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: This section covers domain field options (default, size/value limits, multibyte expansion, pattern matching, whitespace trimming, storage type) and how to override domain definitions with *-prefixed versions, including rules and limitations. It then introduces entity classes: their purpose, attributes, standard key/details/list structs, database vs non-database operations, and entity class options. It also explains optimistic locking, table-level auditing, and exit points (pre/post data access, validation, on-fail), including their parameters and special handling for ns operations.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-44
children.summary: This section covers Cúram modeling rules for entity, extension, facade, process, struct, and attribute classes. It explains exit points and validation, entity inheritance limits, and the Last Updated Field feature. It defines when to use extension classes and their strict constraints. It details facade and process class stereotypes, supported operations, options, and batch/JMS rules. It also describes struct class structure, audit fields, and attribute stereotypes/options, including NULL handling and key ordering.

children.title: Edition — pages 45-54
children.summary: This section covers Cúram modeling reference topics on multibyte expansion factor and, mainly, operation options. It explains general operation rules; struct requirements for batch, database, queue, web service connector, and client-visible operations; and many options such as Audit BI, table-level auditing, Auto ID, Business Date, qconnector JNDI settings, No Generated SQL, On Fail, optimistic locking, order by, pre/post data access, Readmulti_Max/Informational, security, and SQL. It includes syntax, applicability, and examples for overriding auditing and using Business Date.

children.title: Edition — pages 55-64
children.summary: Cúram modeling reference pages 55–64 cover operation options and entity operation stereotypes. It explains transactional, where, and mandatory field options, including nested/fully qualified mandatory fields in aggregated structs. The main content is an overview of entity operations: standard vs non-standard generated SQL, handcrafted SQL, non-key, and batch operations. It details insert, read, and readmulti variants, their stereotypes, when to use them, and generator behavior/parameter rules, including constraints on keys, return structs, null handling, and efficiency/indexing considerations.

children.title: Edition — pages 65-74
children.summary: This section covers Cúram entity database operations: standard, non-standard, non-key, batch, and handcrafted SQL variants. It explains modify/remove behavior, key vs details parameters, generated vs model-defined structs, and when operations affect one row, many rows, or all rows. It also describes batchinsert/batchmodify execution via $execute and batch size limits, plus ns and nsmulti rules for custom SQL, structs-only parameters/returns, joins, complex predicates, and multi-record reads. Includes an nsmulti example.

children.title: Edition — pages 75-84
children.summary: Cúram modeling reference for handcrafted SQL in ns/nsmulti operations: nsmulti examples with one parameter and with key+list parameters, generated wrapper/list structs, Java interfaces, and calling code. Explains host variables, null handling rules, datatype nullability, and DB2 z/OS FOR UPDATE locking guidance. Also covers ns operation examples using wrapper return types and parameter qualifiers. Begins aggregation modeling: one-to-one and one-to-many class/struct aggregation, generated list wrapper behavior, and assignable struct field copying via assign().

children.title: Edition — pages 85-94
children.summary: Cúram modeling reference topics: assignable relationships for generated assign methods, including explicit field mappings, suppressing default same-name assignments, and combining multiple structs via repeated assign calls. It also covers foreign key modeling and DDL generation, including naming rules and key/unique constraints; indexes and unique indexes via entity-to-struct relationships, with generated SQL and unique-constraints XML; and the generated class hierarchy for entities, facades, and processes, including intf/base/impl/factory roles, inheritance, abstract classes, and factory-based instantiation.

children.title: Edition — pages 95-104
children.summary: Cúram modeling reference covering class hierarchy rules and JMS queue connectors. Explains access control, `super`, and enforcing factory use via abstract impl classes. Introduces qconnector operations for facade/process classes: one struct parameter, optional struct return, queue/message settings, and JMS BytesMessage/TextMessage encoding. Lists options (JNDI names, timeout, encoding). Details fixed-length datatype encodings, custom field mappers via `QueueConnectorFieldMappers.properties`, and examples for variable-length fields and list-aggregation mappings.

children.title: Edition — pages 105-112
children.summary: Covers two topics: a custom MQ field mapper for a list of structs and subclass modeling/customization. The mapper example shows QueueConnectorFieldMappers.properties entries, then Java encode/decode logic using a 4-character count header and fixed-length 18-character PersonDtls records. Subclassing is explained for process/facade/entity/wsinbound classes: add/override operations, not attributes; support replacement of superclass, abstract classes, and validation/exit-point overrides. Ends with application customization guidance: keep customizations separate to preserve upgradeability.

Front matter and the main Cúram modeling reference (pages 11–112). Covers UML input meta-model, stereotypes, data types, packages, AuditMappings, domain definitions and overrides, entity/facade/process/struct/attribute/operation rules and options, mandatory fields, entity CRUD/batch/handcrafted SQL operations, aggregation and assign(), foreign keys/indexes, generated class hierarchy and factories, JMS queue connectors and field mappers, subclass modeling, and application customization guidance. Useful as the top-level navigation node for the full modeling reference.

It is mainly for reference on usage rights, privacy policy access, and trademark ownership.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram 8.1.2 modeling reference. It lists the main chapter “Cúram modeling reference” and its subsections, covering UML and input meta-model basics, packages, class types (domain definition, entity, extension, facade, process, struct), attributes and operations, entity CRUD/batch/SQL operations, aggregation, assignable, foreign keys, indexes, unique indexes, generated class hierarchies, JMS queue connectors, subclass modeling, and application customization. Includes page references only; useful as a navigation index, not substantive content.

children.title: 1 Cúram modeling reference
1.1 Cúram modeling overview
children.summary: Introduces the Cúram UML-based modeling approach for building client/server applications with reduced complexity. It explains that the UML meta-model defines domains, entities, processes, structs, and remote interfaces, and that IBM Rational Software Architect is used to edit it. The Cúram Server Code Generator produces Java server code, Java beans, and XML, which the Cúram Data Manager turns into SQL scripts for database creation. Also notes the guide covers model-based functions and mappings to database structures.

children.title: 1.2 UML overview
UML and the input meta-model
children.summary: This section explains that UML constructs created in Rational Software Architect Designer form the input meta-model for the Cúram Generator. The meta-model is a logical system representation made of packages, classes, attributes, operations, and relationships, and it drives generation of Java classes and sometimes database tables and indexes in DDL. It also introduces the Cúram architecture’s main layers, especially the remote interface layer and the business object layer, and briefly describes the BPO as the core place for business logic and entity manipulation.

children.title: Architecture layers
children.summary: This section explains the Cúram architecture and UML input meta-model. It describes how UML constructs created in Rational Software Architect Designer form the input meta-model for the Cúram Generator, including packages, classes, attributes, operations, and generation of Java classes and some DDL tables/indexes. It also outlines the architecture layers: remote interface, business object (BPO), data access, and local interface, with emphasis on the remote layer’s client/middleware role and the BPO as the main place for business logic.

children.title: Stereotypes
Class stereotypes
children.summary: This section introduces Cúram UML stereotypes and explains that they guide generator behavior and output. It focuses on class stereotypes, listing the main supported types: audit_mappings for audit database fields, domain_definition for defining data types, entity for database table maintenance, extension for adjusting audit/last-updated options, and facade for client-visible operations. It also notes related layer descriptions for data access and local interface support.

children.title: Attribute stereotypes
children.summary: Lists Cúram attribute stereotypes and what they mean. It introduces attribute stereotypes as including data items, defaults, and keys, then defines audit_mappings (AuditMappings audit field), data item (attribute on RDO/ListRDO), default (public struct attribute/field), details (non-key entity attribute/field), and key (entity key attribute/field), with references to related sections. It also begins the operation stereotypes table, showing batch and batchinsert.

children.title: Operation stereotypes
children.summary: This section lists Cúram operation stereotypes and their purposes, with references to detailed pages. It includes batch and batchinsert/batchmodify for large or batch-processed data, default for standard non-database operations, standard database operations such as insert, modify, read, readmulti, and remove, non-key variants (nkmodify, nkread, nkreadmulti, nkremove), non-standard SQL variants (ns, nsinsert, nsmodify, nsmulti, nsread, nsreadmulti, nsremove), qconnector for external JMS, and wmdpactivity for deferred processing.

children.title: Relationship stereotypes
children.summary: Defines Cúram relationship stereotypes and related data types. Relationship stereotypes listed here include aggregation, assignable, extension, foreignkey, index, and uniqueindex, each with a brief purpose and pointer to a detailed section. It also introduces the input meta-model’s platform-neutral data types, used for attributes, arguments, and return values, mapped via the SDEJ. The excerpt begins the data type table with SVR_BLOB, a binary type mapped to curam.util.type.Blob, requiring a size qualifier for database fields and allowing nulls.

This section explains Cúram UML stereotypes and how they control generator behavior and output. It covers class stereotypes such as audit_mappings, domain_definition, entity, extension, facade, process, rest, struct, and wsinbound; attribute stereotypes including audit_mappings, data item, default, details, and key; and operation stereotypes like batch, batchinsert/modify, default, standard and non-key database operations, non-standard SQL variants, qconnector, and wmdpactivity. It also begins the platform-neutral data type table used in the input meta-model.

children.title: Data types
children.summary: Explains Cúram input meta-model data types and how they abstract database, middleware, and Java layer types for platform- and database-neutral attributes, arguments, and return values. Introduces the SDEJ mapping tool, then lists core types and constraints: SVR_BLOB, BOOLEAN, CHAR, DATE, DATETIME, DOUBLE, FLOAT, INT8, INT16, INT32, INT64. Notes each type’s Java correspondence, database storage/nullability rules, and special qualifiers such as SVR_BLOB size.

Introduces the UML input meta-model used in Rational Software Architect Designer as the source for the Cúram Generator. It explains that packages contain classes with attributes, operations, and relationships, generating Java code and sometimes DDL tables/indexes. It outlines Cúram architecture layers: remote interface, business object (BPO), data access, and local interface. It then covers stereotypes for classes, attributes, operations, and relationships, showing how they control generation, and ends with platform-neutral data types and their Java/database mappings.

children.title: 1.3 Packages
The CODE_PACKAGE option
Rules for the CODE_PACKAGE feature
children.summary: Defines how CODE_PACKAGE affects generated Java package names for process, facade, entity, and struct classes. Key rules: CODE_PACKAGE must be a valid Java identifier; it applies recursively to subpackages; a child package’s CODE_PACKAGE overrides the parent’s; “.” or “$” mean blank and cannot override a non-blank value; multi-level values like cp1.cp2.cp3 are allowed. Also shows how setting CODE_PACKAGE changes the generated intf/base/fact/impl class locations for a process such as MyProcess.

Explains the CODE_PACKAGE option, which changes generated package names for process, facade, entity, and struct classes. It applies recursively to subpackages, can be overridden by a child package, and must be a valid Java identifier. A dot or $ means blank and cannot override a non-blank value; multi-level values like cp1.cp2.cp3 are allowed. Includes an example showing how MyProcess classes move from the default package into custom.intf/base/fact/impl locations.

This section explains UML package behavior and the CODE_PACKAGE option. Package hierarchy is flattened in generated output, but package-level options apply recursively to subpackages and can be overridden lower down. CODE_PACKAGE affects process, facade, entity, and struct classes, changing generated Java package locations under the default curam package. It must be a valid Java identifier; “.” or “$” means blank and cannot override a non-blank value. Multi-level values like cp1.cp2.cp3 are allowed, with an example showing MyProcess generated into custom.intf/base/fact/impl.

children.title: 1.4 AuditMappings classes overview
AuditMappings classes rules
children.summary: Defines the rules for the `AuditMappings` model class used to enable audit fields on entities. The class must use the `audit_mappings` stereotype, contain only valid domain-definition attributes, and be flat (no aggregation). If present, it exposes audit field mappings that entities can enable via the Audit Fields option. Audit fields store creation time, modification time, program ID, and user ID, and are added to tables and standard entity details structs. A Java implementation is required in the impl package.

children.title: AuditMappings classes outputs
children.summary: Explains how `AuditMappings` classes are used when entity auditing is enabled. Covers required class structure (matching meta-model fields, public fields, no inheritance needed) and optional setter callbacks, plus a warning not to use callbacks to populate fields. Notes that if the class is missing, compilation fails unless you delete the model class or disable auditing with `-noauditmappings`. Also describes automatic audit-field updates in details structs and database, and that operation stereotypes `modify`, `nsmodify`, and `insert` set audit information.

children.title: AuditMappings classes options
children.summary: Explains the two AuditMappings attribute options in Cúram models: Exclude from modify and Exclude from insert. Exclude from modify prevents a field from changing during modify/nsmodify/nkmodify after being set on insert; Exclude from insert prevents setting the field during insert/nsinsert but allows later updates. Both default to false. Notes that ns-stereotype operations cannot exclude audit mapping fields, so handcrafted SQL is needed. Also advises using TransactionInfo.getProgramTimeStamp() for timestamp audit fields.

Explains Cúram `AuditMappings` support for entity auditing. Covers required model rules (stereotype `audit_mappings`, flat class, valid domain attributes) and the need for a matching Java impl class with the same public fields, optional setter callbacks, and no inheritance. Describes how audit fields (creation/modification time, program ID, user ID) are added to tables and standard details structs, how auditing is enabled per entity, and how `modify`, `nsmodify`, and `insert` operations populate audit data. Notes compile failure if the impl is missing unless auditing is disabled.

children.title: 1.5 Domain definition classes
Domain definition class options
Code table names
children.summary: Explains the Code table names option for domain definitions. A code table name lists valid entries for a domain; for hierarchies, use the lowest-level table name. Describes how editable fields show drop-down lists (including n-level lists for hierarchies) and how read-only fields show translations, with only the lowest-level translation shown in hierarchies. Notes this option is only valid for domains defined from a Code Table Root, and that if a root is set to yes, dependent domains must specify a Code Table Name or the generator errors.

children.title: Code table roots
children.summary: Explains the Code table roots option for Cúram domain definitions. A domain marked as a code table root can serve as the base of a code table hierarchy; any domains defined from it must specify a Code table name, or generation fails with an error. The section also notes how code table names drive editable dropdowns and read-only translations, with only the lowest-level translation shown for read-only hierarchies.

children.title: Compress embedded spaces
children.summary: Explains the “compress embedded spaces” option for string domain definitions: it preserves single whitespace between words, removes extra whitespace runs, and trims leading/trailing whitespace before sending to the server. Defines whitespace using `Character.isWhitespace`, notes it can affect multi-line text by removing indentation, and warns results may be unexpected when non-space whitespace starts a run. Also notes the option overrides separate leading/trailing space settings.

children.title: Convert to uppercase
children.summary: Converting to uppercase is a domain definition option that transforms a string field to uppercase before sending it to the server. This section also briefly sits among related validation and text-handling options such as compress embedded spaces, code table names, and custom validation function names. It helps determine whether a domain definition should normalize string case on input.

children.title: Custom validation function name
children.summary: Explains the “Custom validation function name” option for domain definition validation. It lets you specify the name of a developer-supplied function used by client-side domain validation in the application UML model. The value must be only the function name (not class + function) and must be a valid Java identifier. Notes that this feature is deprecated and points to the newer domain plug-in system.

children.title: Default option
children.summary: Defines field/domain validation and defaulting options in Cúram modeling. Covers default option behavior, maximum/minimum size and value constraints, multibyte expansion factor settings for string domains (including override hierarchy and Db2/MBCS implications), and pattern match validation using full-string Java नियमित expressions. Useful for understanding input limits, database/storage effects, and when these options are applied before data is sent to the server.

children.title: Maximum size
children.summary: Describes field validation and storage-related constraints in Cúram modeling. This section explains maximum size as the maximum number of characters allowed before server submission, tied to database storage size and implemented in the client and DDL. It also briefly covers default option, maximum/minimum value and size, multibyte expansion factor for string domains, and pattern match using full Java regular expressions.

children.title: Maximum value
children.summary: Explains the “Maximum value” field constraint: it sets the highest numeric value allowed before the field is sent to the server. This section appears alongside related validation options such as maximum/minimum size and value, multibyte expansion factor, and pattern match. Useful if you need numeric input validation rules or related field constraint settings in Cúram modeling.

children.title: Minimum size
children.summary: Defines the “Minimum size” field constraint: the minimum number of characters that must be entered before submission to the server. The section sits among related validation/settings options such as maximum size, maximum/minimum value, multibyte expansion factor, and pattern match, which are used to control client-side input validation and storage behavior.

children.title: Minimum value
children.summary: Defines the “Minimum value” field constraint: the minimum numeric value allowed before a field is sent to the server. This section appears alongside related field validation options such as maximum size/value, minimum size, multibyte expansion factor, and pattern match. Use this node if you need numeric lower-bound validation details for Cúram modeling reference fields.

children.title: Multibyte expansion factor
children.summary: Describes the multibyte expansion factor for string domains: a float from 1.0 to 4.0 used when MBCS data is stored in Db2/DB2 for z/OS to adjust size calculations. It overrides the global build-time default factor and is used only when needed to avoid Db2 limits; 1.0 disables expansion for the domain. It can be set when data is known to be non-localized. The setting is ignored if multibyte expansion is disabled at build time, and string entity attributes can override the domain value.

children.title: Pattern match
children.summary: Pattern match defines a validation rule for string fields using a regular expression. The entered value must match the entire string before it is sent to the server, not just part of it. The syntax follows standard Java 1.5 regular expressions, with reference to java.util.regex.Pattern documentation for supported features.

children.title: Remove leading spaces
children.summary: Explains string/domain definition options for trimming input and overriding domains in SDEJ. Covers removing leading and trailing spaces before sending strings to the server, choosing storage type for string domains with specified length, and creating override definitions by prefixing a domain name with an asterisk (for example, *PERSON_NAME). Notes that overrides affect all usages, should not change type/code table settings, and can be done only once for existing domains.

children.title: Remove trailing spaces
children.summary: Explains the “Remove trailing spaces” option: trailing spaces are stripped from a string before it is sent to the server. This section sits alongside related string handling settings like removing leading spaces and storage type, and includes broader domain definition override guidance, examples, and limitations in the Server Development Environment.

children.title: Storage type
children.summary: Explains the storage type setting for string domain definitions with a specified length, which controls the string storage data type used in the database. Also notes how domain definition overrides can change a string field’s maximum size or storage type via SDEJ, using an asterisk-prefixed replacement domain, and warns that overrides affect all usages, should not alter types/code tables, and can only be applied once.

Lists domain definition class options and what they do. Covers code table roots/names for hierarchical code-table domains, including editable drop-downs, read-only translations, and generator errors if dependent domains omit a table name. Also explains string handling and validation options: compress embedded spaces, convert to uppercase, custom validation function name (deprecated), defaulting, max/min size and value, multibyte expansion factor for Db2 MBCS, and whole-string pattern match rules. Useful for domain input normalization and validation behavior.

children.title: Overriding a domain definition
Domain definition override examples
children.summary: Explains how to override an existing domain definition in SDEJ without changing the original: create a new domain with the same name prefixed by an asterisk (for example, *PERSON_NAME). The overridden version is used at build time with its own data type and options. Notes that overrides affect all usages, should not change type/code table settings, can only be done once, and cannot be applied to an override or to a non-existent base domain.

children.title: Domain definition override considerations and limitations
children.summary: Explains how domain definition overrides work and their constraints. Overrides are created in SDEJ by defining a same-named domain prefixed with an asterisk (for example, *PERSON_NAME) and are used at build time instead of the original. The section warns that overrides affect all uses of the original domain, so changes can break existing functionality. It notes that changing the domain type, code table name, or code table root is not recommended, and that overrides can only be created once, not overridden themselves, and only for existing domains.

children.title: Domain definition usage rules
children.summary: This section explains domain definition override rules in SDEJ. It covers removing leading/trailing spaces and storage type for string domains, then focuses on how to override an existing domain definition without changing the original. It gives examples such as `*PERSON_NAME`, notes that overrides affect all usages of the original domain, and warns against changing type or code table settings. Key limits: a domain can be overridden only once, overrides cannot be overridden again, and an override requires the original domain to exist.

Explains how to override an existing domain definition in SDEJ without changing the original, using a new domain named with a leading asterisk (for example, *PERSON_NAME). The override is applied at build time and can change options such as maximum string size or storage type. It affects all uses of the original domain, so breaking existing functionality is a risk. Overrides are limited: they can be created only once, cannot be overridden themselves, and require the original domain to exist.

Defines Cúram domain definition classes and their 15 options. Covers code-table domains (root/name rules, editable drop-downs, read-only translations, and generator errors if a dependent domain omits a code table name), string validation/normalization (space compression, uppercase, custom validation, default, size/value limits, MBCS expansion, pattern matching, trimming), storage type, and domain overrides. Overrides use a leading asterisk (for example, *PERSON_NAME), apply at build time, affect all uses of the original domain, and cannot be chained or created without the original domain.

children.title: 1.6 Entity classes
Entity class rules
children.summary: Defines core rules for Cúram entity classes. Entities represent database tables, support operations like read/insert/modify/remove/readmulti, and can have attributes, operations, dependencies, inheritance, and aggregations. Key rules: every entity must have at least one attribute unless it is a subclass, in which case it must have none; entities cannot aggregate other classes. Entity attributes map to table columns, require a nonblank stereotype, and must be either key or details attributes.

children.title: Entity attributes
Details attribute
children.summary: Explains that a details attribute is an entity attribute mapped to a database table column and included in the entity’s standard details struct. It sits within the broader entity class rules: entities correspond to database tables, support operations like read/insert/modify/remove, and attributes generate columns and struct fields. Also notes related constraints on entity attributes, such as the attribute stereotype being required and the prohibition on using certain domain definition overrides as struct/entity attributes.

children.title: Key attribute
children.summary: Explains how a class’s key attribute is mapped in generated artifacts. The key attribute is stored as a database table column, is part of the primary key, and appears in both the standard details struct and the standard key struct. It also introduces entity operation categories: database operations are recognized and generated to access the RDBMS directly, while non-database operations only get prototypes/skeletons and must be implemented in the BOL.

Entity classes map to database tables and support database operations like read, insert, modify, remove, and readmulti. Entity attributes become table columns and are not generated in BOL/RIL, but appear in standard key and details structs. An entity must have at least one attribute unless it is a subclass with none, and entities cannot aggregate other classes. Entity attribute stereotypes cannot be blank; they must be details or key attributes. Domain definition overrides starting with an asterisk cannot be used as struct/entity attributes.

children.title: Entity operations
Database operations
children.summary: Explains how entity classes map to database operations in Cúram. Standard details structs contain all class attributes and are used for inserts, reads, and updates; readmulti returns arrays of these structs. The key attribute is stored as a table column, part of the primary key, and included in both standard details and standard key structs. Entity operations are split into database operations, which are generated and access the RDBMS directly, and non-database operations, which only generate prototypes/skeletons and require manual implementation.

children.title: Non-database operations
children.summary: Explains how entity operations are split into database and non-database operations by stereotype. Database operations are generated to access the related RDBMS table, while non-database operations are only stubbed with prototypes and skeletons; their BOL bodies must be implemented manually. Also notes standard details structs (for all attributes, used in insert/read/update and readmulti returns) and standard key structs (for key stereotype attributes). Mentions operation stereotypes such as batch, nsinsert, and readmulti.

Entity operations are split by stereotype into database and non-database operations. Database operations are generated and act directly on the related RDBMS table, covering standard read/insert/update/delete variants. Non-database operations only generate prototypes and skeletons; their BOL bodies must be implemented manually. The section also explains that standard details structs hold all class attributes for insert/read/update, readmulti returns arrays of them, and the key attribute is stored in the table, part of the primary key, and included in standard details and standard key structs.

children.title: Entity outputs
Standard key structs
children.summary: This section explains how entity classes are transformed into output classes and generated structs. It focuses on standard key structs, which include only key-stereotyped attributes and are used for primary-key operations like read and delete; they are named EntityKey and are generated only when key attributes exist. It also covers standard details structs (all attributes, used for insert/read/update, named EntityDtls) and standard list structs (for readmulti/nkreadmulti, containing a sequence of details structs). It briefly introduces entity class options such as abstract, optimistic locking, audit fields, validation, last updated field, No Generated SQL, and replace superclass.

children.title: Standard details structs
children.summary: Explains how entity classes are transformed into output classes and structs. Standard key structs are generated only when key-stereotyped attributes exist and are used for primary-key operations like read/delete. Standard details structs are generated for all entity classes, include all attributes, and are used for insert/read/update data; arrays of them are returned by readmulti operations. Also notes naming conventions: EntityKey, EntityDtls, and EntityDtlsList. Mentions entity class options such as abstract, optimistic locking, audit fields, validation, last updated field, No Generated SQL, and replace superclass.

children.title: Standard list structs
children.summary: Explains entity outputs and standard structs for entity classes. Entity classes become operation-only classes, with attributes transformed into structs. Covers standard key structs (key attributes only; used for read/delete; named EntityKey), standard details structs (all attributes; used for insert/read/update; named EntityDtls), and standard list structs (for readmulti/nkreadmulti; contains a dtls sequence of details structs; named EntityDtlsList). Also notes entity class options such as abstract, optimistic locking, audit fields, validation, last updated field, No Generated SQL, and replace superclass.

Entity classes are converted into operation-only classes with no attributes; their attributes are moved into generated structs. The section defines standard key structs for key-stereotyped attributes only (used for primary-key read/delete, named EntityKey), standard details structs for all attributes (used for insert/read/update, named EntityDtls), and standard list structs for readmulti/nkreadmulti results (a dtls sequence, named EntityDtlsList). It also lists entity class options: abstract, optimistic locking, audit fields, validation, last updated field, No Generated SQL, and replace superclass.

children.title: Entity class options
Entity class abstracts
children.summary: This section explains how entity classes are transformed into operation-only classes and how their attributes become structs. It covers standard key structs (for key attributes, used in read/delete), standard details structs (all attributes, used in insert/read/update), and standard list structs (for readmulti/nkreadmulti results). It also lists entity class options and defines the entity class abstracts option, which marks a class as abstract and typically intended for subclassing.

children.title: Allow optimistic locking
children.summary: Explains class features for entities and related modeled classes. Covers when to enable optimistic locking, audit fields, validation, and last updated fields, noting that optimistic locking, audit fields, and last updated fields apply only to non-subclass entities. Describes that optimistic locking supports certain database operations and must be switched on per class, validation runs automatically before insert/modify operations, and last updated adds a timestamp field updated on writes except ns operations.

children.title: Audit fields
children.summary: This section explains entity-only configuration options: audit fields, validation, and last updated field. Audit fields add preconfigured audit metadata to an entity and its standard details struct for tracking modification history. Validation defines an exit point that runs before insert/modify operations. The last updated field adds a timestamp updated on writes, except for ns operations. It also notes these features, like optimistic locking, do not apply to subclasses.

children.title: Enable validation
children.summary: Describes the validation feature for entity operations. The validation exit point automatically calls data validation before data-access layer entity operations with stereotype insert or modify. It notes related exit points documentation and frames validation alongside other entity-only features on the page such as optimistic locking, audit fields, and last updated fields, which apply only to non-subclass entity classes.

children.title: Last updated field
children.summary: This section explains the “last updated field” feature for entity classes. It applies only to non-subclass entities and must be explicitly enabled. When switched on, it adds a timestamp field that is typically updated with the current date/time whenever the record is written, except when the write is performed by an ns operation.

children.title: No Generated SQL
children.summary: Explains the entity modeling option “No Generated SQL,” which disables generated SQL/data access code for all database operations of an entity class, allowing custom implementations; individual entity operations can override it. The excerpt also begins surrounding entity options: “replace superclass” for subclass entities and optimistic locking for concurrency control, which uses a version field, is only allowed on entity classes, and affects modify/nkmodify/nsmodify operations.

children.title: Replace superclass
children.summary: Explains the “Replace superclass” option for entity subclasses: if enabled, requests to create the superclass actually create the subclass, letting you change behavior by substituting classes. The section also introduces optimistic locking for entity classes, describing version-based concurrency control, its update failure behavior when another user changes the record, and that it applies to modify/nkmodify/nsmodify operations.

Describes entity class output structures and options. Entity classes generate operations plus standard structs: Key structs for primary-key operations, Details structs for full data (insert/read/update), and List structs for readmulti/nkreadmulti results. Lists available entity options: entity class abstracts, allow optimistic locking, audit fields, enable validation, last updated field, No Generated SQL, and replace superclass. Notes that several options apply only to non-subclass entities; validation runs before insert/modify, last updated adds a timestamp on writes except ns operations, and optimistic locking requires enabling per class.

children.title: Optimistic locking for concurrency control
children.summary: Explains optimistic locking for entity concurrency control: multiple users can read the same record, but only one can commit changes. A version field is added to the table and auto-incremented on each modification; generated DAL code checks it during update and aborts with an exception if the version changed. Notes that locking occurs only during commit, reducing lock time but risking update failure if another user edits first. Applicable only to entity classes; supported by modify, nkmodify, and nsmodify operations.

children.title: Table-level auditing
Information captured by table-level auditing
children.summary: This section explains table-level auditing for database operations. It says auditing is enabled per operation via the Database table-level auditing option and is supported for all stereotyped entity operations except ns, nsmulti, batchinsert, and batchmodify. Audit data is stored in the AuditTrail table. Captured fields include transaction date/time, user ID, modified table name, program name (FID), and transaction type. If optimistic locking is enabled, audit data also includes old and new record versions; otherwise it only records the SQL operation invoked.

children.title: Audit information storage
children.summary: Explains how audit data is stored and what it contains: operation type, key info, and XML-formatted changed data. Describes differences with optimistic locking on vs. off, including before/after comparisons and multi-record updates, and notes unchanged fields may be filtered or included depending on mode. Mentions optional compression via curam.audit.audittrail.datacompressionthreshold and that audit records default to the AuditTrail database table, with support for custom handlers implementing curam.util.audit.AuditLogInterface.

Table-level auditing is enabled per entity operation via the Database table-level auditing option. It is supported for all stereotyped entity operations except ns, nsmulti, batchinsert, and batchmodify. Audited data is written to the AuditTrail table and records transaction date/time, user ID, modified table name, program name (FID), and transaction type. If optimistic locking is enabled, the audit also captures old/new record versions; otherwise it logs only the SQL operation invoked.

children.title: Exit points
Pre-data access
children.summary: Describes audit trail details and pre-data access exit points. Audit logs capture operation type, key info, and changed data in XML; with optimistic locking they record before/after values for a single affected record, otherwise only new field values and no before/after comparison. Auditing data can be compressed via a configuration property and stored by default in the AuditTrail table or handled by a custom AuditLogInterface implementation. Defines pre-data access as an exit point callback executed before the DAL function, after validation, with methods named like preread.

children.title: Post-data access
children.summary: This section explains data access exit points in Cúram. It covers post-data access methods (named post<method>, e.g. postread), the autovalidate function used before standard insert/update and before pre-data access functions, and on-fail handling for data access errors (named onFail<method>). It also summarizes exit point parameter rules, including when method parameters and return types are passed, and notes an nsread/ns limitation where return types are excluded unless workarounds are used.

children.title: Validation
children.summary: Validation is a per-entity exit point named autovalidate, called before standard insert/update and before pre-data access functions. It centralizes validation code and is only usable by insert and modify operations because it receives the entity’s standard details struct. It throws the same exceptions as the entity’s stereotyped operations. The section also explains exit point parameter rules: usually include the method’s parameters and return type, with a noted exception for validate and for nsread/ns operations.

children.title: On-fail
children.summary: Describes the on-fail exit point for data access methods. It runs when a data access function errors, and is named with the method prefix `onFail` (for example, `onFailread`). For non-void operations, the return class is passed in as an argument and is always null. Also summarizes general exit point parameter rules: exit points usually receive the original method parameters and return type, with a special limitation for `nsread` and `ns` operations.

children.title: Exit point parameters
children.summary: Exit point methods generally take the same parameters as the target method plus its return type, if any; extra database-operation parameters are only accessible here. Validate exit points are an exception: they take exactly one parameter, the entity’s standard details struct, and apply only to insert/modify. Return type parameters are not included for nsread and ns operations; a workaround is to use an unstereotyped wrapper method and disable post-data access on the ns operation.

children.title: Exit point uses
children.summary: Exit points should be used for validation or to complete a business process, such as updating an invoice header after changing invoice details. They should not be used to fill in incomplete incoming parameters; instead, wrap the database function in a non-database function that copies and completes the record before calling the operation. Adding an exit point must not affect other users of the operation. The page also introduces entity inheritance: entity classes may subclass only other entity classes, adding operations but not attributes.

children.title: Exit point misuses
children.summary: Exit point guidance: use exit points for validation or to complete a business process, but do not use them to populate incomplete input fields. Instead, wrap the database function in a non-database function that copies the record, fills missing fields, then invokes the database operation. Also ensure any exit point added to an entity operation does not affect other users of that operation.

Defines exit points in Cúram: callback methods executed at strategic points in server DAL operations. Covers four types—pre-data access (pre<method>, runs after validation before DAL), post-data access (post<method>, after DAL), validation (autovalidate, per-entity, before insert/update and pre-data access), and on-fail (onFail<method>, on data access error). Explains that exit point methods usually receive the target method’s parameters and return type, with exceptions for validate and nsread/ns, and notes a workaround for missing return-type parameters.

children.title: Entity inheritance
Entity inheritance usage rules
children.summary: Entity inheritance is restricted to entity classes inheriting only from other entity classes. Subclasses may add any number of extra database or user-defined operations, but cannot add attributes because the underlying relational table must remain unchanged. Entity subclasses also do not generate their own standard key and details; they reuse the base class key and details structs. This section helps determine when entity subclassing is valid and what changes it can safely introduce.

Entity inheritance allows input meta-model entity classes to subclass only other entity classes, typically to add special processing or functionality for the same database table. Subclasses may add extra database or user-defined operations, but cannot add attributes because the underlying relational table must remain unchanged. They also do not generate their own standard key/details; they reuse those from the base class. Use this section to determine when entity subclassing is valid and what changes it can introduce.

children.title: Last updated field
children.summary: Describes the Last updated field feature for entity classes: an automatically added SVR_DATETIME `lastWritten` field that records record modification time for reporting. It’s similar to AuditMappings, available only for entities, and is populated by infrastructure on most writes except ns operations. Explains how to enable it per entity or globally via `Bootstrap.properties`, the required `LAST_UPDATED` domain definition, and which operation stereotypes update it (insert/modify/batch variants, including ns/nk forms).

Entity classes are the Cúram model’s table-mapped building blocks: they expose database operations (read/insert/modify/remove/readmulti), generate operation-only classes plus standard Key/Details/List structs, and enforce rules such as at least one attribute unless subclassed, no aggregation, and key/details stereotypes only. This section also covers entity options (abstract, optimistic locking, audit fields, validation, last updated field, No Generated SQL, replace superclass), exit points, table-level auditing, subclassing rules, optimistic locking behavior, and the last updated `lastWritten` field setup and supported operations.

children.title: 1.7 Extension classes
Extension classes usage
children.summary: Extension classes let you specify options for an existing target class without changing its metamodel. An extension class links to one target class via an “extension” relationship; at build time its contents are superimposed on the target class. In practice, they should be used only to enable Last Updated Field on an entity or Audit Fields on an entity or struct. The section also notes compatibility requirements in RSA Designer and warns that extension relationships are stored as free-standing package objects and may be lost if not placed carefully.

children.title: When to use extension classes
children.summary: Extension classes let you add options to a target class without changing its meta-model; the extension is linked to exactly one target class and is superimposed at build time. Use them only for two cases: enabling the Last Updated Field option on an entity, or the Audit Fields option on an entity or struct. The section also notes that extension classes can carry other model elements in RSA, but only these options are compliant, and the extension relationship must be stored carefully to avoid loss during upgrades.

children.title: Extension classes: considerations and limitations
children.summary: Extension classes let you add or override options on a target class without changing its meta-model; at build time their contents are merged into the target. They must link to exactly one target class via an extension relationship and be created as a stereotype extension in a custom subpackage. In practice, they should only be used to enable Last Updated Field on an entity or Audit Fields on an entity/struct. The section also warns that extension relationships are stored as separate package objects and must be placed carefully to avoid loss during upgrades.

children.title: Extension classes: usage rules
children.summary: This section defines three usage rules for extension classes: an extension class applies to only one target class, a class can have multiple extension classes, and extensions may be applied only to entity or struct stereotyped classes. It also begins the next topic, facade classes, which are business-process wrappers with strict rules and supported operation types (default, batch, wmdpactivity, qconnector).

Extension classes let you add options to an existing class without changing its metamodel. Each extension class links to exactly one target class, and its contents are superimposed on the target at build time. Use them only to enable Last Updated Field on an entity or Audit Fields on an entity or struct; other changes are noncompliant. In RSA Designer, ensure compatible settings and store the extension relationship carefully, since it is a free-standing package object that can be lost during upgrades.

children.title: 1.8 Facade classes
Facade class rules
children.summary: Facade classes represent client-visible business processes in the business object layer. They encapsulate operations that orchestrate entity and process classes, with no data maintenance or database table relationships. Key rules: a facade must have stereotype facade, cannot have aggregations or attributes, can inherit only from other facade classes, and cannot share the same name. The section also introduces facade operation stereotypes: default, batch, wmdpactivity, and qconnector, with batch operations limited to one struct parameter.

children.title: Facade classes operations
default
children.summary: Defines facade classes and their rules, then introduces supported facade operations. Facade classes encapsulate client-visible business processes, manipulate other entity/process classes, and must be stereotyped facade. They cannot have aggregations, attributes, or inherit from entity/process classes, and they cannot share the same name. The section also notes four facade operation stereotypes: default, batch, wmdpactivity, and qconnector. “default” is a plain standard operation, while batch operations generate wrapper code and have limits: at most one parameter, which must be a struct.

children.title: batch
children.summary: Describes batch operations within facade classes. A batch operation is a facade method stereotype that generates source-code wrappers for a batch wrapper program, allowing the Batch Launcher to run it. Key rules: batch operations may have at most one parameter, and that parameter must be a struct. This section sits within facade class rules and operation stereotypes, alongside default, wmdpactivity, and qconnector.

children.title: wmdpactivity
children.summary: This section explains the wmdpactivity stereotype for facade class methods, used to mark a method as a deferred processing method. It also notes related facade class rules and options: a facade class cannot have more than one batch operation, and facade classes can be abstract, generate a facade bean, or replace a superclass. It briefly links wmdpactivity to qconnector and JMS-based integration.

children.title: qconnector
children.summary: qconnector describes a facade operation stereotype used for JMS integration. For operations marked qconnector, the generator creates source code needed to connect to a JMS provider such as IBM MQSeries, enabling Cúram connectors to communicate with other systems through JMS queues. The section sits alongside facade class options (abstract, generate facade bean, replace superclass) and notes a facade class cannot have more than one batch operation.

Facade classes model client-visible business processes as collections of operations in the business object layer. They use stereotype facade, cannot have attributes, aggregations, or inherit from entity/process classes, and cannot share the same name. This section introduces four operation stereotypes: default (standard), batch (generates wrapper code for Batch Launcher; max one struct parameter), wmdpactivity (deferred processing), and qconnector (JMS integration such as IBM MQSeries).

children.title: Facade class options
Abstract
children.summary: This section explains facade class options in Cúram modeling: abstract, generate facade bean, and replace superclass. It notes that abstract classes are meant for subclassing, generate facade bean creates a stateless session bean for external or message-driven access, and replace superclass is used when a facade class was subclassed from another class. It also mentions related JMS connector and batch-operation constraints, including that a facade class cannot have more than one batch operation.

children.title: Generate facade bean
children.summary: This section explains facade class options, especially “generate facade bean,” which creates a stateless session bean so the facade can be accessed by other systems or message-driven beans. It also notes related facade options: abstract classes and replacing a superclass. A key constraint mentioned is that a facade class cannot have more than one batch operation.

children.title: Replace superclass
children.summary: This section explains facade class options, especially “replace superclass.” It says to use this option when the applicable facade class was subclassed from another class. It also briefly notes related facade options: abstract (marks the class abstract) and generate facade bean (creates a stateless session bean for access by other systems or message-driven beans).

This section describes the three facade class options in Cúram modeling: abstract, generate facade bean, and replace superclass. Abstract makes the class subclassable; generate facade bean creates a stateless session bean for access by other systems or message-driven beans; replace superclass is used when the facade class was subclassed from another class. It also notes a constraint: a facade class cannot have more than one batch operation, and mentions deferred-processing and JMS connector-related stereotypes.

Facade classes model client-visible business processes in the business object layer. They orchestrate entity and process classes, have no data maintenance or table relationships, and must be stereotyped facade. Rules: no attributes or aggregations, only inherit from other facade classes, and no duplicate names. Supported operation stereotypes are default, batch, wmdpactivity (deferred processing), and qconnector (JMS integration); batch operations accept at most one struct parameter and a facade can have only one batch operation. Options include abstract, generate facade bean, and replace superclass.

children.title: 1.9 Process classes
Business Process Objects
children.summary: This section defines process classes and Business Process Objects (BPOs) in Cúram. It explains that process classes model business processes by orchestrating entity/process operations rather than accessing the database directly, and that BPOs are the main handcrafted business logic in the Business Object Layer. It lists core process class rules: stereotype must be process, no aggregations, inheritance only from process classes, no attributes, and duplicate names require different CODE_PACKAGE values. It also notes supported operations: default, batch, wmdpactivity, and qconnector.

children.title: Process class rules
children.summary: Process classes model business processes as operations that coordinate entity and process classes, without direct database or RDBMS access. They have the stereotype process and reside in the Business Object Layer. Key rules: no attributes, no aggregations to other classes, and inheritance only from other process classes, not entity classes. Duplicate process class names are allowed only when CODE_PACKAGE differs. Supported process class operations are default, batch, wmdpactivity, and qconnector.

children.title: Process class operations
default
children.summary: This section defines the default stereotype as a standard/plain operation and contrasts it with batch and qconnector stereotypes. It gives key batch-operation rules: at most one parameter, parameters must be structs, and a process class can have only one batch operation. It also notes wmdpactivity as a deferred processing method stereotype. The section introduces process class options, especially abstract classes, and mentions related subclassing guidance and JMS queue connector references.

children.title: batch
children.summary: Defines the batch stereotype for process class operations. Batch operations generate source-code wrappers so the Batch Launcher can run the operation. Key constraints: a batch operation may have only one parameter, that parameter must be a struct, and a process class may contain only one batch operation. The section also briefly cross-references related stereotypes like default, wmdpactivity, and qconnector, and begins process class options (abstract, Generate Function Identifiers, replace superclass).

children.title: wmdpactivity
children.summary: This section explains the wmdpactivity stereotype for process class methods. It states that marking a method with this stereotype designates it as a deferred processing method, and refers readers to the Cúram Server Developer documentation for details. The surrounding page also contrasts related method stereotypes like default, batch, and qconnector, and notes process class options such as abstract, Generate Function Identifiers (FIDs), and replace superclass.

children.title: qconnector
children.summary: This section explains the qconnector operation stereotype for process classes. It states that the generator creates source code to connect to a JMS provider (for example, MQSeries) and points to Cúram JMS queue connectors for details. It appears alongside other process class stereotypes/options such as default, batch, and abstract, with batch rules also described nearby. Open this section if you need JMS connector generation behavior or related process class operation stereotypes.

This section introduces process classes as process-stereotyped business process objects in the BOL that package business logic and do not map to database tables or attributes. It lists core rules: no aggregations, only inherit from other process classes, and duplicate names are allowed only with different CODE_PACKAGE values. It also states the four supported operation stereotypes—default, batch, wmdpactivity, and qconnector—setting up the detailed behavior and constraints for each.

children.title: Process class options
Abstract
children.summary: This section describes process class operation stereotypes and options in Cúram modeling. It explains default, batch, wmdpactivity, and qconnector stereotypes, including generator behavior and batch operation constraints. It then covers process class options such as abstract, Generate Function Identifiers (FIDs), and replace superclass. The abstract option marks a class as abstract and intended for subclassing; subclassing is noted for process, facade, entity, and wsinbound classes.

children.title: Generate Function Identifiers (FIDs)
children.summary: This section states that a Generate Function Identifiers (FIDs) class requires a FID to be generated, and notes that replace superclass applies only when the process class was subclassed. It also introduces struct classes: Java classes with public attributes and no methods, used to group domain data and as operation parameters. Key struct rules include: non-empty, no operations, valid domain types, may aggregate entities/structs, cannot inherit, flat for certain database parameters, require distinct CODE_PACKAGE if similarly named, and standard key/details structs are auto-generated.

children.title: Replace superclass
children.summary: Describes the “Replace superclass” option, which applies only when the process class has been subclassed, and refers to subclass modeling for details. Also briefly notes that subclassing can be used for process, facade, entity, and wsinbound classes to add or override functionality. The page then begins the Struct classes section, explaining that structs are Java record-like classes with public attributes, no operations, used for operation parameters and aggregations, and listing core struct rules and restrictions.

Covers process class operation stereotypes and options in Cúram modeling. It explains default operations plus batch, wmdpactivity, and qconnector stereotypes, including batch wrapper generation and constraints: one parameter max, parameter must be a struct, and only one batch operation per process class. It also introduces process class options: abstract (for subclassing), Generate Function Identifiers (FIDs), and replace superclass. Subclassing is noted for process, facade, entity, and wsinbound classes to add or override functionality.

Process classes are BOL business process objects that encapsulate business logic as sequences of entity/process operations, without direct database or RDBMS access. This section defines their core rules: stereotype must be process, no attributes or aggregations, and inheritance only from other process classes; duplicate names require different CODE_PACKAGE values. It also introduces supported operation stereotypes—default, batch, wmdpactivity, and qconnector—and process class options such as abstract, Generate Function Identifiers (FIDs), and replace superclass.

children.title: 1.10 Struct classes
Struct class rules
children.summary: Struct classes are Java record-like classes with public attributes and no modeled methods, used to group domain values into parameters or programmatic records. This section defines eight rules: structs must contain at least one attribute/aggregation, cannot have operations or inheritance, their attributes must use valid domain definitions, and they may aggregate entities or other structs. Structs used as key/details parameters for non-standard database operations must be flat. Same-named structs are allowed only if CODE_PACKAGE differs; standard key/details structs are auto-generated.

children.title: Struct class outputs
children.summary: This section explains struct class outputs in Cúram modeling: input meta-model struct classes generate Java classes in the `<ProjectPackage>.<CodePackage>.struct` package, with public fields for each attribute, default initialization, comments for datatype hierarchy, and clone/assignment support. Struct classes have no DDL counterpart. It also covers the struct class option for enabling audit fields, which adds preconfigured audit metadata for use in audited entity write operations. The section ends by introducing attributes and class/attribute stereotype rules.

children.title: Struct class options
Audit fields
children.summary: This section explains the “audit fields” option for struct classes. Enabling it automatically adds preconfigured audit fields to a generated Java struct in the `<ProjectPackage>.<CodePackage>.struct` package, allowing the struct to be used for write operations on entities that already have audit fields enabled. It notes that audit fields store record modification history and can be added to database tables for auditing.

This section describes struct class options for generated Java struct classes in the `<ProjectPackage>.<CodePackage>.struct` package. It explains that struct fields are generated with default values, include domain-hierarchy comments, support cloning/assignment, and have no DDL counterpart. The main option covered is audit fields: enabling it adds preconfigured audit fields to the struct so it can be used for write operations on entities with audit fields already enabled, storing modification history for auditing.

Struct classes are Java record-like classes with public attributes and no modeled methods, used to package domain values and other structs into operation parameters. This section defines eight rules: structs must be non-empty, have no operations or inheritance, use valid domain types, may aggregate entities or structs, and must be flat when used as key/details parameters for non-standard database operations. Same names are allowed only with different CODE_PACKAGE values; standard key/details structs are auto-generated.

children.title: 1.11 Attributes
Attribute rules
children.summary: This section introduces struct classes and attribute rules in the Cúram modeling reference. It explains that input meta-model struct classes generate Java classes in the `<ProjectPackage>.<CodePackage>.struct` package, with public fields for each attribute, default initialization, cloning/assignment support, and no DDL counterpart. It also covers the optional audit fields struct class feature. The attributes subsection states that class stereotypes determine valid attribute stereotypes and that class-attribute stereotype combinations control how the generator processes the UML meta-model.

children.title: Attribute options
Allow NULLs
children.summary: Explains the “Allow NULLs” attribute option for entity class details attributes. It controls whether the generated database column permits NULLs; setting it to no adds a Not Null qualifier in generated DDL. The option is only available for details-stereotyped attributes on entity classes. Default values vary by datatype: no for boolean, char, float, double, money, int8, int16, int32; yes for blob, date, datetime, string, int64. Notes that some Cúram datatypes may be stored as null when empty in handcrafted SQL.

children.title: Multibyte expansion factor
children.summary: Defines the multibyte expansion factor override for string entity attributes, used with DB2/DB2 for z/OS MBCS data. The factor is a float from 1.0 to 4.0 and can override the domain-level setting and interact with global build-time properties. It is only needed to deviate from global/domain defaults, may be set to 1.0 to effectively disable expansion for nonlocalized Western-only strings, and is ignored if multibyte expansion is turned off globally. Includes references to related concepts and nearby operation rules.

Defines attribute options for entity classes: only details-stereotyped attributes can use “Allow NULLs” or multibyte expansion factor. “Allow NULLs” controls whether the generated database column is nullable; setting it to no adds a NOT NULL qualifier in DDL. Defaults depend on datatype (no for boolean/char/float/double/money/int8/int16/int32; yes for blob/date/datetime/string/int64). It also notes some handcrafted SQL may store empty Cúram values as null.

This section defines attribute rules and options in the Cúram modeling reference. It maps class stereotypes to valid attribute stereotypes, requires unique Java-valid, domain-definition-based attributes, and notes that entity primary-key order follows attribute order. It also covers attribute options, especially “Allow NULLs” for entity details attributes: it controls DDL nullability, with datatype-based defaults, and mentions handcrafted SQL may store empty values as null. It also notes struct classes expose generated Java fields with defaults, cloning/assignment support, no DDL, and optional audit fields.

children.title: 1.12 Operations
Operation rules
children.summary: This section defines operation rules in Cúram modeling. It states that operations must belong to entity, process, facade, or wsinbound classes, may be handcrafted or generated using standard operation features, and cannot be hidden/exposed individually—only at the class level. It also specifies struct usage rules: batch operation parameters must be structs, and all database operation parameters and return types must be structs.

children.title: Operation options
Audit Business Interface (BI) calls to this operation
children.summary: Explains the “Audit Business Interface (BI) calls to this operation” option for Cúram operations. It applies only to client-visible operations and controls BI-level auditing of operation calls, recording the function identifier, caller username, date/time, and transaction type. Notes that this setting can be overridden at application startup via application properties. The section sits within operation options and is followed by related options like table-level auditing, Auto ID field/key, and Business Date.

children.title: Auto ID field
children.summary: Explains operation options for Cúram, focusing on Auto ID field and related settings. Auto ID field applies only to certain entity-class insert operations and auto-populates a field with a generated unique ID. Auto ID key selects the key set used to generate that ID and is only used with Auto ID field. The section also introduces Business Date, which marks one operation parameter as the business date for process-class operations, and notes prior constraints on parameter/return types for different operation stereotypes.

children.title: Auto ID key
children.summary: This section explains operation options for Cúram process/entity operations, focusing on auditing and ID generation. It covers Business Interface auditing for client-visible operations, database table-level auditing, the Auto ID field for insert operations, and the Auto ID key used with Auto ID Field to generate a unique ID from a specified key set. It also introduces the Business Date option for process class operations, where a parameter value becomes the transaction’s business date.

children.title: Business Date
Syntax for the Business Date option
children.summary: Explains the Business Date option for batch programs, which lets runs use a specified processing date instead of the system date. It gives examples of why this is useful for rerunning reports for past dates. It also defines the syntax for setting the option: either `fieldName` or `paramName.fieldName`, where the parameter name is optional if it is the first operation parameter. Includes examples using `effectiveDate` and `paymentDate` in batch operation structs.

children.title: Rules for the Business Date option
children.summary: This section defines when the Business Date option applies and how it behaves. It applies only to individual server transactions from facade and batch operations, not workflow activity or deferred processing. It must use an SVR_DATE or SVR_DATETIME field, and only affects remote client or Batch Launcher invocations; direct Java calls ignore it. Null/zero date values do not override the transaction date. The excerpt also briefly notes BytesMessage encoding for process-class connectors and database table-level auditing for entity-class database operations.

Defines the Business Date operation option for process-class operations. It marks one parameter field as the transaction’s business date, so batch processing can run against a specified processing date instead of the system date. The section explains its value for rerunning date-sensitive batch jobs, and gives syntax examples for `fieldName` or `paramName.fieldName` using batch structs such as `effectiveDate` and `paymentDate`.

children.title: BytesMessage encoding character set
children.summary: The section defines when the BytesMessage encoding character set option applies: only to connector operations in process classes. It points readers to qconnector operation options for details. The surrounding text also notes related runtime options such as Business Date and database table-level auditing, including their scope and applicability to specific operation types. Open this node if you need the exact applicability rules for BytesMessage encoding or nearby operation-option behavior.

children.title: Database table-level auditing
children.summary: This section explains database table-level auditing for entity-class database operations. It defines table-level auditing as recording changes made to actual database table data, notes that it only applies to entity class database operations, and says its behavior depends on whether optimistic locking is enabled. It also mentions the option can be overridden at application startup via application properties, including for Audit BI.

children.title: Field Level Security
children.summary: Field Level Security lets you secure individual fields returned by client-visible facade operations. In Rational Software Architect Designer, configure it on the facade operation’s Secure Fields tab by assigning a security identifier (SID) to each returned field. This applies only to client-visible operations in facade classes, not other operation types.

children.title: JNDI name of the QueueConnectionFactory class
children.summary: Explains the JNDI name setting for the QueueConnectionFactory used only in qconnector operations of process classes. It points readers to the qconnector operation options for details and says to use the appropriate qconnector operation. Also notes a related setting for the transmission queue JNDI name, which applies in the same way.

children.title: JNDI name of the transmission queue
children.summary: Describes the JNDI name setting for the transmission queue used by qconnector operations in process classes. Notes that this option applies only to qconnector operations and refers readers to the qconnector operation options for details. Useful for configuring messaging/queue connectivity in process definitions.

children.title: JNDI name of the reply queue
children.summary: This section defines the JNDI name of the reply queue as a configuration field for qconnector operations, with references to related qconnector options. It also notes that message type applies only to qconnector operations of process classes. The remainder introduces the No Generated SQL option for entity class database operations, explaining that it suppresses generated data access code so you must implement methods yourself, including special handling for readmulti operations via the ReadmultiOperation visitor pattern.

children.title: Message type
children.summary: Explains the “Message type” setting for qconnector operations, noting it applies only to process class qconnector operations and refers to the qconnector operation options. The excerpt then transitions to “No Generated SQL,” which is a database-operation option for entity classes that suppresses generated data access code so you can provide handcrafted implementations. It includes examples for standard read and readmulti operations, emphasizing the ReadmultiOperation visitor pattern for returning records.

children.title: No Generated SQL
children.summary: Explains the No Generated SQL option for entity-class database operations, which suppresses generated data access code so you can supply your own implementation. Shows that standard read operations generate an abstract `<name>_da` method to implement. Notes that readmulti-style operations use a different visitor pattern: the returned list is ignored, and records must be added via `ReadmultiOperation.operation(Object)` rather than returned directly.

children.title: On Fail operation
children.summary: Explains the On Fail operation option for entity-class database operations. It enables an on-fail exit point that runs when a DAL error occurs, receiving copies of the original parameters and the corresponding DAL exception. The handler can either manage the error there or rethrow it for elsewhere. Also notes this option applies only to database operations of entity classes and is related to exit points and optimistic locking options.

children.title: Optimistic locking
children.summary: This section explains the Optimistic locking option for entity-class database operations. It states that optimistic locking is only available for certain update operations and only if the entity class has Allow Optimistic Locking enabled. The section sits alongside related DAL customization topics such as On Fail operations and exit points, and links to more detailed concurrency-control guidance.

children.title: Order by
children.summary: Describes the Order by option for entity read operations. It applies only to readmulti, nsreadmulti, and nsreadmulti stereotypes, lets you choose any entity fields for sorting, and always sorts ascending. If omitted, returned records are in arbitrary order.
