The excerpt also briefly introduces post-data access and pre-data access options for database exit points, and Readmulti_Max, which limits records returned by readmulti-like operations, with 0 meaning no limit.

children.title: Post-data access operation
children.summary: Explains the post-data access option for entity database operations: it indicates whether a standard database operation has a post-exit point (callback) executed by the server. Applies only to entity-class database operations. This section sits alongside related concepts such as pre-data access, exit points, and other readmulti-related controls, but the core focus here is detecting post-exit-point support.

children.title: Pre-data access operation
children.summary: Defines the pre-data access operation for entity-class database operations: it indicates whether a pre-exit point exists and applies only to entity database operations. The section is adjacent to related topics on post-data access, ordering, and Readmulti_Max. It notes that exit points are callback functions executed at predefined points by the server, with a link to more details on exit points.

children.title: Readmulti_Max
children.summary: Explains the Readmulti_Max option for entity operations: it sets the maximum number of records returned by readmulti-style reads. Applies to readmulti, nsreadmulti, nkreadmulti, and nsmulti. If more records exist, behavior depends on Readmulti_Informational; without that setting, Readmulti_Max is not enforced. If Readmulti_Informational is unspecified, the generator default is used. A value of 0 means no limit (infinity).

children.title: Readmulti_Informational
children.summary: Explains the Readmulti_Informational operation option for entity operations with stereotypes readmulti, nsreadmulti, nkreadmulti, and nsmulti. By default, when Readmulti_Max is reached, a Readmulti_Max message is logged and all entries are returned. If enabled, an InformationalMessage is added to the transaction’s InformationalManager and only the specified maximum number of entries is returned. The default is false. Also briefly notes nearby operation options for qconnector timeout, security, and SQL.

children.title: Response message timeout (seconds)
children.summary: Applies only to qconnector operations in process classes. The response message timeout (seconds) option controls the timeout behavior for qconnector responses; see qconnector operation options for details.

children.title: Security
children.summary: Explains the Security operation option for entity operations. It applies only to client-visible records; when enabled, generated RIL code checks whether the user is authorized to invoke the operation and throws an exception if not. Also briefly notes the SQL option for non-standard entity operations, which supplies handcrafted SQL converted into DAL code.

children.title: SQL
children.summary: Explains the SQL option for entity operations. It lets you supply custom SQL executed by the operation; the generator converts it into DAL (Java and SQL) code. Applies only to entity operations with stereotypes ns and nsmulti. Also points to the overview on using handcrafted SQL in non-standard entity operations.

children.title: Transactional
children.summary: Explains the transactional operation, which controls whether a transaction starts for an operation and applies only to client-visible operations. Also introduces the where operation for adding a custom SQL WHERE clause used by DAL-generated SQL, but only for readmulti and nsreadmulti entity classes. This page begins the “Operation parameter options: mandatory fields” section, describing mandatory fields for parameters and that the mandatory option takes a single-line, comma-delimited string.

children.title: Where
children.summary: Defines the entity operation option “where,” used to add a custom SQL WHERE clause to generated DAL SQL for an operation. It applies only to client-visible `readmulti` and `nsreadmulti` operations. The page appears in the operation options reference alongside `transactional`, and transitions into mandatory field options for operation parameters.

Operation options lists 24 Cúram operation settings and their scope. It covers auditing (Audit BI calls, database table-level auditing, runtime overrides via application properties), ID/date controls (Auto ID field/key, Business Date), connector settings for qconnector operations (BytesMessage charset, JNDI names, message type, timeout), security, SQL/no-generated-SQL customization, optimistic locking, exit-point hooks (pre/post-data access, On Fail), sorting and limits (Order by, Readmulti_Max, Readmulti_Informational), and field-level security. Useful if you need exact applicability rules for operation stereotypes.

Operations are modeled functions for entity, process, facade, and wsinbound classes, and may be handcrafted or generator-supported; they can only be hidden/exposed at the class level. Structs are mandatory for batch, database, queue, web service connector, and client-visible operations. The section then details 24 operation options, including auditing, Auto ID, Business Date, qconnector settings, security, SQL/no-generated-SQL customization, optimistic locking, exit points, ordering, Readmulti limits/informational handling, and field-level security.

children.title: 1.13 Operation parameter options: mandatory fields
children.summary: Explains the “mandatory fields” operation parameter option. This option marks parameter fields that must be populated on a client page and applies to any parameter. The value must be a single-line, comma-delimited string. The section introduces an example operation signature and related parameter structures (PersonDetails, AddressDetails, EmploymentDetails) used to illustrate mandatory field specification.

children.title: 1.14 Entity operations overview
Standard operations
Standard single-record operations
children.summary: Explains standard single-record entity operations in Cúram. These are the simplest database operations: the database returns only one row, and no explicit arguments need to be modeled because the generator assumes standard key and details structs where appropriate. This follows the broader overview of entity operations, where recognized stereotypes generate data-access code, while unrecognized operations are left to custom implementation.

children.title: Standard multi-record operations
children.summary: Standard multi-record operations handle multiple rows rather than a single database row. They are used in cases like maintenance UIs that return multiple records for user selection and batch programs that process many table rows, and are represented by the `readmulti` stereotype. The section also introduces non-standard generated SQL operations, which use custom argument/return structs (subsets of entity fields) and may use non-unique keys, with stereotypes `nsinsert`, `nsmodify`, `nsread`, `nsreadmulti`, and `nsremove`.

Introduces Cúram entity operation stereotypes recognized by the generator. Recognized operations get generated data-access Java code; unrecognized ones only get interfaces/factories and require custom implementation. This node covers standard operations, especially standard single-record operations: the database returns one row and no explicit arguments need modeling because the generator assumes standard key/details structs.

children.title: Non-standard operations
Generated SQL operations
children.summary: This section explains generated SQL operations as non-standard Cúram operations. Unlike standard insert/modify/read/remove/readmulti, they use user-defined or generated structs for arguments and returns rather than standard key/details structs. Struct attributes must be subsets of entity fields. It warns that non-unique key structs can cause unexpected behavior, e.g., nsmodify may update multiple matching records. The operation stereotypes listed are nsinsert, nsmodify, nsread, nsreadmulti, and nsremove.

children.title: Handcrafted SQL operations
children.summary: This section describes handcrafted SQL operation types in Cúram. It explains that non-standard handcrafted SQL operations are the most flexible generator-supported option, letting you define custom parameters and SQL; only provided parameters are generated and replicated across layers. Use them when standard operations are insufficient, such as for joins or aggregate queries. It also lists stereotypes for non-standard (`ns`, `nsmulti`), non-key (`nkmodify`, `nkread`, `nkreadmulti`, `nkremove`), and batch operations (`batchinsert`, `batchmodify`), and briefly references insert operations.

This section introduces non-standard Cúram operations and distinguishes two types: generated SQL and handcrafted SQL. Generated SQL operations use user-defined or generated structs for arguments/returns instead of standard key/details structs; struct attributes must be subsets of entity fields, and non-unique keys can cause operations like nsmodify to affect multiple records. It also lists the generated SQL stereotypes: nsinsert, nsmodify, nsread, nsreadmulti, and nsremove.

children.title: Non-key operations
children.summary: Non-key operations are generator-supported database operations that act on all rows in a table, typically used for tables with only one row. The supported stereotypes are nkmodify, nkread, nkreadmulti, and nkremove. The section contrasts these with non-standard handcrafted SQL operations (ns, nsmulti), which allow custom SQL and parameters for cases like joins or aggregates, and notes separate batch-only operation stereotypes (batchinsert, batchmodify).

children.title: Batch operations
children.summary: Batch operations are operations tailored for batch programs. The section states that batch programs use batch-specific operation stereotypes, namely batchinsert and batchmodify. It sits alongside descriptions of non-standard handcrafted SQL operations (ns, nsmulti) and non-key operations (nkmodify, nkread, nkreadmulti, nkremove), which may help distinguish batch from other generator-supported operation types.

Explains entity operation stereotypes recognized by the Cúram generator. Standard operations include insert, modify, read, remove for single-record access and readmulti for multi-record access; these use generated key/details structs and may need no explicit arguments. Non-standard generated SQL operations require explicit argument/return structs whose attributes must be entity-field subsets, and may act on non-unique keys. Stereotypes listed: nsinsert, nsmodify, nsread, nsreadmulti, nsremove.

children.title: 1.15 Entity insert operations
Standard insert
Standard insert description
children.summary: Explains standard insert operations, which use the stereotype insert to create a single database record from an automatically generated standard details struct. No input arguments need to be specified in the model, though extra arguments and exit points are allowed and do not affect generated code. Notes that standard inserts update each attribute and can be used with Auto ID Field sequence number generation. Briefly contrasts with non-standard inserts (nsinsert), which use a non-standard details struct, may leave unspecified attributes null, and can be more efficient but more complex.

children.title: Standard insert use
children.summary: Explains standard insert operations, which use the `insert` stereotype to create a single new database record from an automatically generated standard details struct. No input arguments are required in the model, though extra arguments and exit points can be added without affecting generated code. Use this pattern to create a new record and update each attribute, including with Auto ID Field sequence number generation. Contrasts standard insert with non-standard insert (`nsinsert`), which is more efficient but leaves unspecified attributes null.

children.title: Standard insert parameter and generator notes
children.summary: Explains standard insert operations in Cúram modeling: they use a generated entity details structure as the input parameter, insert a single record, and update all attributes. No input parameters or return value are defined in the model; extra arguments and exit points are allowed but do not affect generated code. Notes that standard inserts are appropriate for creating new records, can be used with Auto ID Field sequence numbering, and contrasts them with non-standard inserts, which use a non-standard details struct and set unspecified fields to null.

Describes standard insert operations (`insert` stereotype) in Cúram modeling: they create one database record using an automatically generated standard details struct, with no input arguments or return value defined in the model. Extra arguments and exit points may be added but do not affect generated code. Standard inserts update all attributes and can be used with Auto ID Field sequence numbering. The section also contrasts them with non-standard inserts (`nsinsert`), which allow unspecified fields to be null and may be more efficient but more complex.

children.title: Non-standard insert (generated SQL)
Non-standard insert description
children.summary: Non-standard insert operations use the nsinsert stereotype to insert a single record into a parent entity’s database table from a non-standard details struct. Unlike standard inserts, unspecified attributes are written as nulls, so they do not update every attribute. This pattern is more efficient due to reduced database I/O, but adds complexity. Use it when the database can perform the insert more efficiently or for high-volume transactions.

children.title: Non-standard insert use
children.summary: Describes the non-standard insert operation (stereotype nsinsert) for generated SQL. It inserts a single record into the parent entity’s database table using a non-standard details struct, with unspecified attributes stored as null. Compared with standard insert, it updates only selected attributes and is more efficient due to reduced I/O, but adds complexity. Recommended when the database can perform the insert much more efficiently or for high-volume transactions.

children.title: Non-standard parameter and generator notes
children.summary: Explains non-standard insert operation notes: the input is a single “details” struct whose fields must match entity attributes by name and type; omitted fields are left uninitialized and become null in the DBMS. A warning is shown if the details parameter omits fields that cannot be null. Also lists parameter/return/generator notes: one non-standard details struct, no return value, no generator action, with a related null-considerations reference.

Non-standard insert operations (stereotype `nsinsert`) insert one record into a parent entity’s table using a non-standard details struct. Unlike standard inserts, unspecified attributes are stored as null, so not all attributes are updated. This reduces database I/O and can improve performance, but increases complexity. The section explains when to use this pattern: when the database can perform the insert more efficiently or for high-volume transactions.

This section explains entity insert operations in Cúram, which add a single row at a time. It covers standard `insert` operations, which use an automatically generated details struct, require no model-defined input or return value, update all attributes, and can work with Auto ID Field sequencing. It also covers non-standard `nsinsert` operations, which use a non-standard details struct, leave unspecified fields null, and can be more efficient but more complex, especially for high-volume or DB-optimized inserts.

children.title: 1.16 Entity read operations
Standard read
Standard read description
children.summary: Standard read operations retrieve a single database record into a standard details struct, using the standard generated key struct (the primary key) as the search criteria. The section also notes that extra arguments and exit points may be specified in the input model, but they do not affect generated code.

children.title: Standard read use
children.summary: Explains standard read operations for entities with primary keys. A standard singleton read uses the entity’s primary key to return one record’s details; its key/details structs are auto-generated and not specified in UML, though extra arguments and exit points may be added. Also covers standard readmulti: uses a designer-specified partial key, returns a list of details structs for matching records, and may be unordered unless an Order By option is set.

children.title: Standard read parameter and generator notes
children.summary: Explains standard read and readmulti generator behavior. Standard singleton read uses an entity primary key to return one record; only entities with primary keys can use it, and the generator auto-adds key and details structs, with no UML-specified parameters. Standard readmulti uses a partial key struct to return a list of details structs; the first parameter is user-specified but the return is auto-generated. Notes include efficiency of indexed access and optional Order By sorting in ascending order.

Standard read operations retrieve a single database record into a standard details struct using the entity’s generated primary key struct as the search criteria. Extra arguments and exit points can be specified in the input model, but they do not affect generated code. This section is part of entity read operations and helps distinguish standard singleton reads from other read types such as readmulti or non-standard reads.

children.title: Standard readmulti
Standard readmulti description
children.summary: Standard readmulti operations use a designated key structure as input and return a list of standard details structures for all matching entity records. The first parameter is required but auto-generated, so it is not specified in the UML meta-model. Records are returned in arbitrary order unless an Order By option is used to sort selected entity fields ascending. The section also notes that readmulti should use efficient database indices, similar to other keyed access operations.

children.title: Standard readmulti use
children.summary: Explains standard readmulti operations: a stereotype-based multiple read that returns all matching records for a specified partial key. It uses automatically generated structures, with the first parameter designated as the key input and a generated list of details as the output. Records are returned in arbitrary order by default, but Order By can sort them ascending by selected entity fields. Also notes that efficient index use is important for keyed access.

children.title: Standard readmulti parameter and generator notes
children.summary: Explains standard read operations for entities with primary keys, especially readmulti. A readmulti uses a designated partial key struct to return a list of matching standard details structs; the input parameter is specified, while the return structure is generated automatically. Results are unsorted by default and can be ordered ascending by entity fields using the Order By option. Notes that efficient indexed access is required, as with other keyed operations.

Standard readmulti defines a stereotype-based multiple-read operation for entities with primary keys. It uses a designated partial key struct as the input parameter and returns a generated list of standard details structs for all matching records; the first parameter is required but omitted from the UML meta-model. Results are unsorted by default and can be ordered ascending by selected entity fields using Order By. The section also emphasizes efficient use of database indices for keyed access.

children.title: Non-standard read (generated SQL)
Non-standard read description
children.summary: Explains non-standard read operations in Cúram generated SQL. They use a user-defined key struct to retrieve a single record into a details struct, often for non-primary-key lookups or partial attribute reads. Notes that the key must uniquely identify one record; otherwise a runtime error/exception occurs. Covers efficiency considerations and the need for appropriate database indices. Also mentions parameter/return structure requirements in the UML meta-model, and briefly introduces non-standard readmulti, which returns a list of matching records.

children.title: Non-standard read use
children.summary: Explains non-standard read operations (stereotype nsread) and when to use them: to read a subset of attributes or use a non-primary key. Covers that they use custom key and details structs, must uniquely identify one record or a runtime error occurs, may be more efficient due to less I/O, and require appropriate indices. Notes parameter/return conventions and generator behavior. Also briefly introduces non-standard readmulti, which uses a partial key and returns a list of matching details records.

children.title: Non-standard parameter and generator notes
children.summary: Explains non-standard read and readmulti operations in Cúram generated SQL. Non-standard read uses a custom key struct to retrieve a single record into a non-standard details struct; the key must uniquely identify one record, or an exception occurs. These structures must be created in the UML meta-model, with attributes matching entity attributes by name and type, though standard generated key/details structs can also be used. Non-standard readmulti uses a partial key plus details struct and returns a list of all matching records.

This section explains Cúram generated SQL non-standard read operations (stereotype nsread). It covers when to use them: reading a subset of attributes or using a non-primary key. It notes that the key must uniquely identify one record, otherwise a runtime error/exception occurs, and that appropriate indices are needed for efficiency. It also describes required UML meta-model key/details structures, generator behavior, and briefly introduces non-standard readmulti, which uses a partial key and returns all matching records as a list.

children.title: Non-standard readmulti (generated SQL)
Non-standard readmulti description
children.summary: Explains non-standard readmulti operations (stereotype nsreadmulti) in Cúram 8.1.2. These operations use a partial key struct plus a details struct as input and return a list of the provided details struct for every matching record. Useful when you need to read multiple records by non-primary-key criteria. Related notes in the section also contrast non-standard read with standard read, covering efficiency, risk of multiple matches, and the need for matching key/details structures and appropriate indices.

children.title: Non-standard readmulti use
children.summary: Non-standard readmulti reads a subset of entity attributes for multiple records using a designer-defined key structure. Unlike standard readmulti, it requires a custom return struct whose fields must be a subset of the entity; the generator wraps it in a list. The designer should ensure key fields use database indices efficiently. The section also introduces non-key reads (stereotype nkread), which read a single-row control table without a key parameter; they error at runtime if more than one row exists.

children.title: Non-standard readmulti parameter and generator notes
children.summary: Explains non-standard readmulti operations: unlike standard readmulti, they require a return struct explicitly defined in the UML meta-model, and its fields must be a subset of the entity’s fields. The key-struct input also must use entity fields only. The generator wraps the specified details struct in a list for the operation return. Also covers non-key read operations (stereotype nkread), which read a single row into a standard details struct, take no key parameter, and are intended for single-record control tables; multiple rows cause a runtime error.

Explains non-standard readmulti (stereotype nsreadmulti) in Cúram 8.1.2. These generated SQL operations take a partial key struct and a details struct, and return a list of the details struct for every matching record. Useful when reading multiple records by non-primary-key criteria or a subset of entity attributes. The section also contrasts non-standard read with standard read, noting efficiency benefits, the risk of multiple matches, and the need for matching key/details structs and appropriate indices.

children.title: Non-key read
Non-key description
children.summary: Non-key read (nkread) operations read a single table row into a standard details struct without taking a key parameter. They execute SQL with no WHERE clause, so they operate on all rows and require the table to contain exactly one record; otherwise a runtime error occurs. This is typically used for control tables with a single row. There is no non-key insert operation because inserts do not need a key.

children.title: Non-key use
children.summary: Non-key read operations (stereotype `nkread`) read the single record in a table into a standard details struct and do not take a key parameter. They run SQL without a WHERE clause, so they operate on all rows and require the table to contain exactly one row; otherwise a runtime error occurs. This is typically used for control tables with a single record.

children.title: Non-key parameter and generator notes
children.summary: Explains non-key read operations and generator behavior. Non-key singleton reads take no parameters; the generator adds the entity’s standard details struct as the return type, and the operation throws an exception if more than one row exists. Non-key readmulti operations (stereotype nkreadmulti) read all rows in a table without a key or where clause, returning a list wrapper of standard details structs. Notes that non-key inserts do not exist and that these are typically used for control tables or reading all records.

Non-key read (`nkread`) retrieves the only row in a table into the standard details struct and takes no key parameter. It generates SQL without a WHERE clause, so it operates on all rows and requires the table to contain exactly one record; otherwise a runtime error occurs. It is typically used for single-row control tables. There is no non-key insert operation because inserts do not require a key.

children.title: Non-key readmulti
Non-key readmulti description
children.summary: Explains non-key readmulti operations (stereotype `nkreadmulti`): they behave like standard readmulti but do not take a key and return all rows from a table, with no WHERE clause. Intended for reading all records/attributes, typically from small or control tables. The UML meta-model does not specify parameters; the generator creates a list wrapper of the entity’s standard details struct as the return value. Notes that there is no non-key insert operation, and contrasts with non-key singleton reads.

children.title: Non-key readmulti use
children.summary: Explains non-key readmulti operations (stereotype `nkreadmulti`) for reading all rows of a table without a key or `where` clause. Unlike non-key singleton read, it returns a list wrapper containing the entity’s standard details structs. UML meta-model specifies no parameters; the generator adds the list wrapper return type automatically. Useful when you need to retrieve all attributes from every record in a table rather than a partial-key match.

children.title: Non-key readmulti parameter and generator notes
children.summary: Explains non-key read operations, especially nkreadmulti. Non-key singleton reads take no parameters, require exactly one table row, and the generator adds the entity’s standard details struct as the return value. Non-key readmultis behave like readmulti without a key or WHERE clause, returning all rows in a list of standard details structs. The UML meta-model does not specify parameters; the generator creates a list wrapper for the standard details struct. Mentions that non-key inserts do not exist.

Explains non-key readmulti operations (`nkreadmulti`): they work like standard readmulti but take no key and no WHERE clause, so they return all rows from a table. Intended for reading all records/attributes, often from small or control tables. The UML meta-model specifies no parameters; the generator adds a list wrapper of the entity’s standard details struct as the return value. Notes that non-key singleton reads require exactly one row, and that non-key insert operations do not exist.

Covers entity read operation types: standard read/readmulti, non-standard read/readmulti, and non-key read/readmulti. Explains when each is used, their key and details struct requirements, and generator behavior. Standard read uses the primary key and returns one record; standard readmulti returns a list for a partial key and can be ordered. Non-standard reads use custom key/details structs, can read subsets of attributes or non-primary keys, and require uniqueness for singleton reads. Non-key reads use no key and read all rows, typically for single-row control tables or full-table lists.

children.title: 1.17 Entity update operations
Standard modify operation
Standard modify operation description
children.summary: Describes the standard modify entity update operation. It updates a specific database record using the entity’s primary key, so it always affects a single row and cannot be used for entities without primary keys. No input arguments are specified in the UML model; the generator adds standard key and details structs automatically. Extra arguments may be passed for exit points but do not affect generated code. It updates all attributes of the target record and can be used with optimistic locking.

children.title: Standard modify operation use
children.summary: Standard modify operations update all attributes of a single existing database record identified by the entity’s primary key. They require no input-model arguments; the generator supplies standard key and standard details structs automatically, and extra arguments can be passed only for exit points with no effect on generated code. This pattern cannot be used for entities without primary keys and can be combined with optimistic locking.

children.title: Standard modify parameter and generator notes
children.summary: Standard modify operations update a single database record identified by an entity primary key. This section notes that the operation uses generated standard key and standard details structs as input; these parameters are added by the generator and are not defined in the UML meta-model. There are no explicit input parameters or return value, though extra arguments may be passed for exit points without affecting generated code. It also states standard modify cannot be used for entities without primary keys and can be combined with optimistic locking.

Standard modify is a modify-stereotyped entity update operation that updates all attributes of one existing database record using the entity’s primary key, so it always affects a single row and cannot be used for entities without primary keys. It takes no UML-defined input parameters or return value; the generator automatically supplies standard key and standard details structs. Extra arguments may be passed only for exit points and do not affect generated code. It can also be used with optimistic locking.

children.title: Non-standard modify (generated SQL)
Non-standard modify description
children.summary: Explains the `nsmodify` non-standard modify operation, which updates one or more database records using a custom key and custom details struct. It can update a subset of entity attributes, may affect multiple rows, and can be more efficient than standard modify operations due to reduced I/O and fewer index updates. Notes that input key/details structures must be defined in the UML meta-model and match entity attributes by name and type. Also distinguishes `nkmodify` non-key modify operations, which update all rows and have no key parameter.

children.title: Non-standard modify use
children.summary: This section explains non-standard modify (nsmodify) operations: they update one or more matching database records using a user-defined non-standard key and a non-standard details struct. They can update a subset of entity attributes and may be more efficient than standard modify due to reduced database I/O and index updates. It also notes UML parameter requirements: custom key/details structs must match entity attributes by name and type, though standard generated structs can also be used. A brief comparison to non-key modify is included.

children.title: Non-standard parameter and generator notes
children.summary: This section explains non-standard modify operations (`nsmodify`) and related generator notes. It says these operations update one or more database records using user-defined non-standard key and details structs, which must be declared as UML operation parameters and match entity attributes by name and type. It notes that standard generated key/details structs can also be used. It also introduces non-key modify operations (`nkmodify`), which update all rows on a table without a key/where clause and are typically used with single-row control tables.

Describes `nsmodify` generated SQL operations for updating one or more table records using user-defined non-standard key and details structs. They can update only a subset of entity attributes, may affect multiple rows, and can be more efficient than standard modify due to less I/O and fewer index updates. The key/details structs must be UML operation parameters and match entity attributes by name and type; standard generated structs may also be used. Also introduces `nkmodify`, which updates all rows without a key/where clause, typically for single-row control tables.

children.title: Non-key modify
Non-key modify description
children.summary: Non-key modify operations (stereotype nkmodify) update all rows in a database table using a standard generated details struct, without any key parameter. They execute SQL with no WHERE clause, so they affect every record on the table. This section also notes that non-key read is only appropriate when the table has a single row, and that there is no non-key insert because inserts do not require a key.

children.title: Non-key modify use
children.summary: Non-key modify updates all rows in a database table by setting each record’s attributes to values supplied in the operation parameter. It is typically used only for control tables with a single record. The entity’s details structure is automatically generated as the input parameter and is not defined in the UML meta-model. This section also begins the entity delete operations overview, introducing remove, nsremove, and nkremove, and then describes standard remove as a primary-key-based delete of a single record.

children.title: Non-key parameter and generator notes
children.summary: Explains non-key modify operations: they update all rows in a table using values from the entity details structure, with no UML-specified parameters or return value; the generator adds the standard details struct automatically. Also begins the entity delete operations section, introducing standard remove: deletes one record by primary key, requires a generated key structure, cannot be defined for entities without primary keys, and has no parameters or return value.

Non-key modify (nkmodify) updates all rows in a table using a standard generated details struct, with no key parameter. It runs SQL without a WHERE clause, so every record is affected. The section notes this is only suitable for single-row control tables in related non-key read usage, and that there is no non-key insert because inserts do not need a key.

Entity update operations cover three patterns: standard modify, non-standard modify (`nsmodify`), and non-key modify (`nkmodify`). Standard modify updates one record by primary key using auto-generated standard key/details structs; it has no UML-defined parameters or return value and can use optimistic locking. Non-standard modify uses user-defined key/details structs to update one or more matching rows, often only a subset of attributes, and can be more efficient. Non-key modify updates all rows with no key or WHERE clause, typically only suitable for single-row control tables.

children.title: 1.18 Entity delete operations
Standard remove
Standard remove description
children.summary: Explains the standard remove entity delete operation. A standard remove (stereotype `remove`) deletes one specific database record using the entity’s primary key via an automatically generated standard key struct. It applies only to entities with primary keys and always affects a single row. The section notes that no input-model parameters are needed, extra arguments may be passed for exit points without affecting generated code, and the generated input parameter comes from the entity’s key structure.

children.title: Standard remove use
children.summary: Standard remove deletes one specific database record using the entity’s primary key. It applies only to entities with a primary key, since the key uniquely identifies a single row. The operation uses an automatically generated key structure as input, so no parameters are defined in the UML meta-model, and it returns nothing. Extra arguments may be added for exit points but do not affect generated code.

children.title: Standard remove parameter and generator notes
children.summary: Explains the standard remove entity delete operation. A remove deletes one specific database record using the entity’s primary key, so it can only be defined for entities with a primary key and always affects a single row. The section notes that no input parameters are specified in the UML meta-model because the generator creates the standard key structure automatically; return value is none. It also mentions that extra arguments may be defined for exit points but do not affect generated code.

Standard remove describes the `remove` entity delete operation. It deletes a single database record identified by the entity’s primary key, so it applies only to entities with primary keys. The input is an automatically generated standard key struct; no UML meta-model parameters are defined and the operation returns nothing. Extra exit-point arguments may be supplied, but they do not affect generated code.

children.title: Non-standard remove (generated SQL)
Non-standard remove description
children.summary: Explains the non-standard remove operation (stereotype `nsremove`) for deleting records from the parent entity’s table using a user-provided key struct in the UML model. Notes that the key’s attributes must match entity attributes by name and type, multiple rows may be deleted if the key is not unique, and appropriate database indices are needed for efficiency. Includes a DB2 for z/OS note about negative JDBC row counts causing `RecordNotFoundException`. Also briefly contrasts this with non-key remove operations, which delete all rows with no `WHERE` clause.

children.title: Non-standard remove use
children.summary: Explains the “non-standard remove” generated SQL operation (stereotype nsremove). It deletes one or more rows from a parent entity’s database table using a user-defined key struct, which must match entity attributes by name and type. If the key is non-unique, multiple records are deleted. Notes that efficiency depends on proper database indexes and mentions DB2 for z/OS behavior where a negative JDBC row count can trigger RecordNotFoundException. Also briefly introduces “non-key remove” as deleting all rows with no key/where clause.

children.title: Non-standard remove parameter and generator notes
children.summary: Explains non-standard remove operations (stereotype `nsremove`) and their generator behavior. These deletes target parent entity table rows by a UML-defined key struct whose attributes must match entity attributes by name and type. If the key is non-unique, multiple rows are deleted; proper indexing is recommended for efficiency. Notes DB2 z/OS/JDBC behavior where a negative row count can raise `RecordNotFoundException`. Also briefly lists parameters, return value, and generator action, then introduces non-key remove (`nkremove`) as delete-all-rows without a where clause.

Describes the generated SQL “non-standard remove” operation (stereotype `nsremove`). It deletes one or more rows from the parent entity’s table using a user-defined key struct; the key’s attributes must match entity attributes by name and type. Non-unique keys can delete multiple rows, so proper database indexing is important. Notes DB2 for z/OS JDBC behavior where a negative row count can raise `RecordNotFoundException`. Briefly contrasts this with non-key remove (`nkremove`), which deletes all rows with no `WHERE` clause.

children.title: Non-key remove
Non-key remove description
children.summary: Non-key remove operations, marked with stereotype nkremove, delete all records from a database table. They do not take a key parameter and generate SQL without a WHERE clause, so they operate on every row in the table. This section contrasts them with non-standard remove operations, which delete specific records based on a supplied key structure.

children.title: Non-key remove use
children.summary: Non-key remove operations delete all records from a database table and take no parameters, return no value, and require no generator action. This section also notes that non-key reads are for single-row tables and that non-key inserts do not exist because inserts don’t require a key. It then introduces entity batch operations, especially batchinsert, which groups many inserts to reduce database round trips and improve performance, but requires an explicit $execute call or pending batched inserts are discarded.

children.title: Non-key remove parameter and generator notes
children.summary: Non-key remove operations delete all records from a database table and take no parameters, return no value, and require no generator action. The section also begins entity batch operations, introducing batchinsert and batchmodify as ways to insert or remove many records efficiently. Batch inserts buffer statements locally via PreparedStatement.addBatch and require an explicit $execute call; pending batched inserts are discarded if the entity is destroyed, so batches cannot span multiple online client invocations.

Non-key remove operations (stereotype nkremove) delete all records from a database table. They take no key parameter, return no value, and generate SQL with no WHERE clause, so they affect every row. This section contrasts them with non-standard remove operations, which delete only matching records based on a supplied key structure.

Covers entity delete operations: standard `remove`, non-standard `nsremove`, and non-key `nkremove`. Standard remove deletes one record by primary key using an auto-generated key struct; it requires an entity primary key and returns nothing. Non-standard remove deletes one or more matching rows using a user-defined key struct whose attributes must match entity attributes; indexing matters, and DB2 z/OS may raise `RecordNotFoundException` on negative row counts. Non-key remove deletes all rows in a table with no `WHERE` clause.

children.title: 1.19 Entity batch operations
batchinsert
batchinsert description
children.summary: Batch insert operations insert many records efficiently by batching SQL statements locally, reducing database round trips and improving performance. They have a similar signature to non-standard insert operations and are invoked the same way, but inserts are not written immediately; they are added via `PreparedStatement.addBatch`. The batch must be explicitly executed with the operation’s `$execute` method, which is never called automatically. Pending batched inserts are discarded if the entity object is destroyed, so batch operations cannot span multiple client invocations in online mode.

children.title: batchinsert use
children.summary: Explains batchinsert operations for inserting many records to the same entity in one transaction. Describes the queued batch execution model, including the generated $execute() method returning int[] counts per statement and possible EXECUTE_FAILED or constraint-related zero results. Covers batch size limits via curam.db.batch.limit or per-operation $setBatchSize(int), with AppException thrown if exceeded. Notes that batchinsert takes one non-standard details struct whose fields must match entity attributes, and that the generator adds $setBatchSize() and $execute() methods.

children.title: batchinsert parameter and generator notes
children.summary: Explains batchinsert behavior and generated API. A batch insert takes one non-standard details struct whose fields must match entity attributes by name and type; it inserts many records for the same entity in one transaction. The generator adds $setBatchSize(int) to override curam.db.batch.limit (default 30) and $execute() returning int[] with per-statement update counts, including 1 for success, 0 for no rows/constraint issue, or EXECUTE_FAILED. Warns about nullable fields in the details struct and notes batch-size-limit exceptions.

Batchinsert operations queue multiple inserts for one entity in a single transaction to reduce database round trips and improve performance. Calls resemble non-standard insert operations, but each insert is added via PreparedStatement.addBatch and is not written until the generated $execute() is called explicitly. The batch size is controlled by curam.db.batch.limit (default 30) or $setBatchSize(int); exceeding it throws INFRASTRUCTURE.ID_BATCH_SIZE_LIMIT_HAS_BEEN_REACHED. The single input is a non-standard details struct matching entity attributes by name and type. $execute() returns int[] update counts.

children.title: batchmodify
batchmodify description
children.summary: Describes the batchmodify stereotype and its use for updating many existing records of the same entity in one transaction. Explains that it uses non-standard key and details input structures, which must be defined in UML and match entity attributes by name and type; standard generated structures are also allowed. Notes a warning for non-standard details missing nullable fields. Summarizes generator methods for setting batch size and executing the batch, and warns batchmodify cannot span multiple client-server invocations.

children.title: batchmodify use
children.summary: Defines the batchmodify stereotype and explains that it updates many existing records on the same entity in one transaction. Notes that it uses non-standard key/details input structures (or standard generated ones) that must match entity attributes by name and type, and warns about null-handling for non-standard details. Lists generator methods for setting batch size and executing the queued batch, and states batchmodify cannot span multiple client-server invocations; it must execute within a single online transaction.

children.title: batchmodify parameter and generator notes
children.summary: Explains batchmodify operations, which update many existing records in one transaction using non-standard key and details input structures. Notes that these structures must be defined in the UML meta-model and match entity attributes by name and type; standard generated structs are also allowed. Warns that non-standard details parameters lacking non-null fields trigger a warning. Describes generated methods for batch size and execution, and states batchmodify cannot span multiple client-server transactions. Includes return value and null-considerations reference.

Defines the batchmodify stereotype for updating many existing records on the same entity in one transaction. It uses non-standard key and details input structures (or standard generated ones) that must be defined in UML and match entity attributes by name and type; non-standard details missing non-null fields trigger a warning. Notes generated methods for setting batch size and executing the queued batch, with batchmodify limited to a single online/client-server transaction.

This section defines Cúram entity batch operations: batchinsert and batchmodify. It explains that both queue multiple statements to reduce database round trips, must be explicitly executed with $execute(), and cannot span multiple client invocations because pending work is discarded when the entity object is destroyed. It covers batch size control via curam.db.batch.limit or $setBatchSize(int), the returned int[] update counts, and the exception thrown when limits are exceeded. It also details required input structures, UML/generator notes, and warnings for missing non-null fields.

children.title: 1.20 Entity handcrafted SQL operations
Non-standard
Non-standard description
children.summary: Defines non-standard (ns) handcrafted SQL entity operations. ns operations require struct parameters and a struct return value because the DAL only supports structs; SQL must be supplied manually and no SQL is auto-generated. The operation must belong to an entity class, but its SQL may target any table, including joins across tables. Use ns for complex single-record database work such as non-equals comparisons, multi-table queries, aggregates like max/count, and updates/deletes, as long as no cursor is needed; otherwise use nsmulti.

children.title: Non-standard use
children.summary: Explains when to use a non-standard (ns) entity operation for handcrafted SQL that is too complex for standard operations but does not retrieve multiple records. ns operations require explicit SQL, belong to an entity class, and can query any table or join across tables. Parameters and return values must be flat structs only, with zero or many parameters allowed. Suitable cases include non-equality comparisons, multi-table queries, and aggregate-like results such as max/count. If a cursor is required, use nsmulti instead.

children.title: Non-standard parameter and generator notes
children.summary: This section defines non-standard parameter/generator rules and the non-standard multi stereotype (nsmulti). It states that non-standard multi operations allow zero or one parameter only, must return a struct, and must execute readmulti SQL. They are used for joins across multiple tables or for complex selection criteria beyond simple equals comparisons. It also notes that no extra parameters can be added, and the generator creates a list wrapper for the specified return-value struct for list-details operations.

Defines handcrafted SQL entity operations with two stereotypes: ns and nsmulti. Ns operations require struct parameters and a struct return value, must belong to an entity class, and need explicit SQL (no auto-generation). They can target any table or join, support zero or many flat-struct parameters, and are for complex single-record work like non-equals comparisons, multi-table queries, aggregates, updates, and deletes. Use nsmulti when the SQL needs a cursor or multi-record retrieval.

children.title: Non-standard multi
Non-standard multi description
children.summary: Non-standard multi (stereotype nsmulti) defines a special entity operation for readmulti SQL against multiple tables. It is similar to non-standard operations but has strict limits: zero or one parameter only, must return a struct, and cannot take extra parameters. It is used when joining two or more tables or when selection criteria are too complex for readmulti/nsreadmulti, such as non-equality comparisons. The designer must supply the SQL, and the generator creates a list wrapper for the return-value struct.

children.title: Non-standard multi use
children.summary: This section explains when to use non-standard multi (nsmulti) operations and their modeling restrictions. Such operations must return a struct, allow zero or one parameter only, and require SQL that performs a readmulti. They are used to retrieve data from multiple tables via joins or when selection criteria are too complex for readmulti/nsreadmulti (for example, non-equality comparisons). The designer must provide the SQL directly, unlike nsreadmulti, which is useful for deciding whether to inspect this node.

children.title: Non-standard parameter and generator notes
children.summary: This section describes non-standard operation generator constraints and introduces non-standard multi operations. It notes that standard non-standard parameter operations allow only an optional key parameter, return a list-details struct, and generate a list wrapper, with no extra parameters allowed. For non-standard multi (stereotype nsmulti), the operation can have zero or one parameter, must return a struct, and its SQL must perform a readmulti. It is used for joins across multiple tables or complex selection criteria beyond simple equals comparisons.

children.title: Example 1: nsmulti with a single (list) parameter
children.summary: Example of an `nsmulti` operation with one list parameter, `getMinorTransactions()`, used to list all transactions with amount under $1. It shows the model structs (`MinorTxDetails` with `txDate`, `txAccountNumber`, `txAmount`), relevant `BankAccount` entity attributes, and the SQL you supply (`SELECT ... FROM BankAccount WHERE txAmount < 1`). Also explains that the generator creates the remaining code, including a generated list wrapper `MinorTxDetailsList`.

children.title: Example 2: nsmulti with two parameters (key + list)
children.summary: Explains “nsmulti” with two parameters: a key plus a list result. It shows using a struct wrapper for the account number input because database operations require struct parameters, even for a single field. The example queries minor transactions under one dollar for a specific account, notes that attribute names need not match exactly for ns/nsmulti, and provides the SQL, generated Java interface, and pseudocode for the `MinorTxDetails`, `MinorTxDetailsList`, and `AccountNoWrapper` structs.

Non-standard multi (stereotype nsmulti) defines a custom readmulti entity operation across one or more tables. It is only for joins or complex criteria (for example, non-equality comparisons) and requires designer-supplied SQL. Constraints: zero or one parameter only, no extra parameters, and the operation must return a struct; the generator creates a list wrapper for that struct. Examples show single-parameter and key+list variants, including wrapper structs and generated Java interface/call patterns.

children.title: Using handcrafted SQL in non-standard entity operations overview
Using host variables
children.summary: Explains how to use host variables in handcrafted SQL: prefix them with a colon, keep names case-sensitive, and use the aggregation role name when referencing aggregated fields. Also covers “null” handling: many Cúram types store empty values as database nulls, so searches should use `IS NULL` rather than empty strings. Notes that object-like Java types map to nullable DB values, while primitive types are non-null by default unless the database field’s Allow NULLs option is changed.

children.title: “Null” considerations
Incorrect "null" search
children.summary: Explains SQL host variables and “null” handling in handcrafted Cúram SQL. Host variables must use a colon prefix and be case-sensitive, and aggregated fields use the aggregation role name. For data types stored as null when empty, queries must search with `IS NULL`, not an empty string. Also notes that Java primitive types cannot be null in the database unless the Allow NULLs option is explicitly changed, which inversely controls NOT NULL in generated DDL.

children.title: Correct "null" search
children.summary: Explains how to use host variables in handcrafted SQL and how Cúram maps empty values to database nulls. The key point is that when searching for empty string–backed Cúram datatypes, the query should use `IS NULL` rather than `= ''`. It notes host variables must be prefixed with `:` and are case-sensitive, and aggregation fields use the aggregation role name. Also clarifies that primitive Java types cannot be null by default, with the Allow NULLs option controlling generated NOT NULL constraints.

Explains handcrafted Cúram SQL host variables and null handling. Host variables must be prefixed with a colon, are case-sensitive, and use the aggregation role name for aggregated fields. For Cúram datatypes stored as empty-state nulls, searches must use `IS NULL` rather than `= ''`. Also notes that class-based Cúram types map empty values to database nulls, while primitive Java types do not unless the Allow NULLs option is changed, which inversely controls generated NOT NULL constraints.

children.title: Update considerations with DB2 for z/OS
children.summary: Explains DB2 for z/OS update-locking differences. Handcrafted SQL that uses a FOR UPDATE clause may need modification to avoid RecordLockedException errors, especially when the statement can be run concurrently. It recommends using FOR UPDATE WITH RS USE AND KEEP UPDATE LOCKS so DB2 for z/OS locking matches distributed DB2 behavior. The section then begins an SQL example showing an Employer CountEmployers method and a LongWrapper struct needed because stereotyped entity operations cannot return primitive types.

children.title: SQL example 1
children.summary: This section covers SQL update-lock considerations for DB2 for z/OS and gives an SQL example. It notes that handcrafted SQL using FOR UPDATE may need to change to FOR UPDATE WITH RS USE AND KEEP UPDATE LOCKS to avoid RecordLockedException and match distributed-platform locking behavior. It also includes a data type/nullability table and introduces an example entity method CountEmployers for Employer, explaining that a Serializable DeepCloneable LongWrapper struct is needed because stereotyped entity operations cannot return primitive types.

children.title: SQL example 2
children.summary: Shows a Java/SQL example using parameter host variables in a Cúram interface. It adds `setEmployerSize(EmployerKey empKey, LongWrapper newSize)` alongside `countEmployers()`, defines `EmployerKey` with `employerNumber`, and demonstrates an UPDATE on `Employer` setting `size = :2.longValue` where `employerNumber = :employerNumber`. Highlights how `INTO`/host variable references map to input and return structures.

Explains how to write handcrafted SQL for non-standard entity operations (ns, nsmulti) in Cúram DAL. Covers host variables in SQL: prefix with colon, case-sensitive, and use aggregation role names for aggregated fields. Describes null handling: many Cúram types store empty values as DB nulls, so search with IS NULL, not ''. Notes DB2 for z/OS UPDATE locking may require FOR UPDATE WITH RS USE AND KEEP UPDATE LOCKS. Includes SQL examples for countEmployers and setEmployerSize using return/input structs and host variables.

Defines handcrafted SQL entity operations in Cúram DAL: ns and nsmulti. Ns requires struct parameters and a struct return, belongs to an entity class, and uses designer-supplied SQL for complex single-record work, joins, aggregates, updates, and deletes; no SQL is auto-generated. Nsmulti is for readmulti-style queries, with zero or one parameter only, a struct return wrapped by a generated list, and no extra parameters. Also covers host-variable syntax, null handling, DB2 z/OS update locking, and SQL examples for count and update methods.

children.title: 1.21 Aggregation
A special case
children.summary: This section explains aggregation in Cúram modeling, including one-to-one and one-to-many relationships and their main use in representing sequences in the input meta-model. It notes that structs can aggregate structs or entities. A special case is that standard details structs can be aggregated even though they do not appear in the input model; instead, the entity that owns the standard details struct is aggregated. It then begins a one-to-one aggregation example using PersonDetails and PersonDetailsWrapper.

children.title: One-to-one aggregation
children.summary: Explains one-to-one aggregation in Cúram modeling: embedding a single instance of one class within another. Uses PersonDetails aggregated into PersonDetailsWrapper as the example. Describes how to create the relationship in Rational Software Architect Designer: add both classes, draw an aggregation arrow from wrapper to detail, choose Create Aggregation, select the relationship, and open General Properties. This section is useful for understanding how the generator represents nested structures via one-to-one aggregation.

children.title: One-to-many aggregation
children.summary: Explains how to model a one-to-many UML aggregation in Rational Software Architect Designer using PersonDetailsList and PersonDetails. Describes creating the aggregation, placing the diamond on the composite outer class (PersonDetailsList), and setting multiplicities to * for the list and 1..* for PersonDetails, with role dtls and Composite/None aggregation settings. Notes that the generated code produces a list-valued embedded structure with helper methods for accessing PersonDetails items.

Explains Cúram aggregation for embedding one type within another, mainly to represent sequences in the input meta-model. Covers supported cases: structs can aggregate structs or entities, plus the special case of standard details structs via their owning entity. Describes one-to-one aggregation with PersonDetails inside PersonDetailsWrapper, including RSA Designer steps, role/diamond placement, multiplicity 1, Composite vs None, and generated embedded-code shape. Also covers one-to-many aggregation with PersonDetailsList and PersonDetails, using multiplicity * and 1..*, and generated list-helper pseudo-code.

children.title: 1.22 Assignable
Explicit field assignment
children.summary: Explains explicit field assignment using an assignable relationship to control automatic assignment when field names differ or to suppress default same-name assignment. Describes that explicit mappings are made by linking attributes on both classes; unmapped fields remain default-assigned. Uses Address and BankBranchStruct as examples of classes with differently named fields, noting that attributes such as cityID can be explicitly matched in the relationship.

children.title: Suppressing default assignment fields
children.summary: Explains how Cúram’s generated assignment methods map common fields between linked structs, including an example where `Address.cityCode` is explicitly assigned to `BankBranch.cityID`. It shows that generated `assign()` can replace many manual field copies with a single call. The section also describes suppressing default assignment fields by omitting one end of a relationship when two struct classes (e.g., `PersonInfo` and `AccountInfo`) share same-named fields that should not be auto-matched.

children.title: Combining structs
children.summary: This section explains how to combine multiple structs by populating one struct from the fields of two or more others. It gives a Java example where a `BankBranchStruct` is filled from `AddressDtls` and `BankBranchDtls`, showing field-by-field assignment from one source struct (address lines, city, country, postal, region, version number). It also notes that assignment behavior can exclude keyed fields in earlier examples, affecting which fields are copied.

Explains Cúram struct assignment via generated assign() methods. Covers automatic copying of matching fields, explicit field mapping for differently named attributes (e.g., BankBranchStruct.cityID ↔ Address.cityCode), and suppressing unwanted default matches by omitting a field/key. Also shows combining multiple structs into one target struct, such as populating BankBranchStruct from AddressDtls and BankBranchDtls. Useful for reducing manual field-by-field Java assignment.

children.title: 1.23 Foreign keys
Adding a foreign key to a database table
children.summary: Explains how to define foreign keys in Cúram database models. A foreign key is created by adding a foreignkey relationship between two entity classes, optionally naming the relationship to control the database constraint name. Rules: only entity classes can use foreign keys, referenced fields become unique unless they are already the primary key, and subclass entities cannot define them. The section begins describing how to add key/qualifier mappings on the referencing role in a class diagram.

children.title: Primary and foreign key naming constraints
children.summary: Explains how Cúram names database constraints for keys. Foreign key relationship names in the model are used as the foreign key constraint name unless suppressed with `-nonamedforeignkeyconstraint`. Primary key constraints are named after their entity, creating a same-named index, unless suppressed with `-nonamedprimarykeyconstraint`. Includes a BankAccount/BankTransaction example showing a foreign key from `txAccountNo` to `accountNo` and the resulting Oracle DDL.

children.title: Foreign key example
children.summary: Explains foreign key naming and constraints in Cúram models, including optional suppression of named foreign/primary key constraints via generator flags. Uses an example with BankAccount and BankTransaction where BankTransaction.txAccountNo references BankAccount.accountNo, illustrating key attributes and the resulting Oracle DDL foreign key statement.

Explains how to define Cúram database foreign keys between entity classes using a foreignkey relationship plus key/qualifier mappings in the class diagram. Notes that foreign keys apply only to entity classes, cannot be on subclasses, and referenced fields become unique unless they already are primary keys. Covers naming: the relationship name becomes the FK constraint name unless suppressed, and primary key constraints are named after their entity unless suppressed. Includes a BankAccount/BankTransaction example and resulting Oracle DDL.

children.title: 1.24 Indexes
Adding an index to a database table overview
children.summary: This section explains how to add database table indexes in Cúram using the generator. Indexes are defined in the input model by creating a relationship with stereotype index between an entity class and a struct class whose fields are a subset of the entity’s attributes. It lists constraints: the relationship must be named, struct attributes must match entity attributes, structs cannot aggregate other classes, and index names must be unique. It also notes indexes are not explicitly referenced, so names should be descriptive.

children.title: Index naming overview
children.summary: Use meaningful, descriptive names for index relationships because indexes are never referenced directly in application code, but the database administrator needs them. Indexes are created by adding a relationship of stereotype index between an entity and a struct, and the relationship name becomes the database index name. Index names must be unique across the entire model.

children.title: Index example
children.summary: Explains how to define database indexes in Cúram using a relationship of stereotype index between an entity class and a struct class. Covers rules: the relationship must be named, struct attributes must be a subset of the entity’s attributes, the struct cannot aggregate other classes, and index names must be unique across the model. Notes that structs used for indexes have no side effects and can still be used as operation arguments. Includes guidance to choose descriptive index names and introduces an example index relationship.

Explains how Cúram generator creates database table indexes beyond the primary index. Indexes are modeled as a named relationship with stereotype index between an entity and a struct whose attributes are a subset of the entity’s, with no aggregation allowed in the struct. Index names must be unique across the whole model and should be descriptive because indexes are not referenced directly in application code, though they matter to DBAs. Includes guidance on using the struct as an operation argument and an example relationship.

children.title: 1.25 Unique indexes
children.summary: Explains how to model unique indexes in Cúram: add a unique index stereotype relationship between an entity class and a struct class, using the same modeling rules as non-unique indexes. Generated unique-index information goes into `<Application-name>_unique_constraints.xml`, which must be referenced from `datamanager_config.xml`. Notes that this file also includes implicit unique constraints for foreign-key-referenced fields, with DDL for both explicit unique indexes and automatically generated unique constraints.

children.title: 1.26 Generated class hierarchy
Basic hierarchy example
children.summary: Explains the generated class hierarchy for a basic entity class in Cúram. A modeled entity like MyClass maps to four Java classes: intf (public interface), base (generated abstract implementation with data access and connector methods), impl (handcrafted subclass for custom methods, abstract and not directly instantiated), and fact factory (generated newInstance() used to create objects). Notes that generated and handcrafted code must be kept separate, and that the factory can also return customized or proxy versions.

children.title: Subclasses hierarchy example
children.summary: Explains how a subclass can replace its superclass and shows the generated/handcrafted Java class hierarchy for entity class SubClass inheriting from MyClass. Lists the four resulting classes (intf, base, impl, factory) and the extra inheritance relationships created by subclassing: the interface extends the superclass interface, and the base class inherits the handcrafted superclass impl so methods are reused. Also introduces the abstract-class variant, where a class cannot be instantiated and the hierarchy changes accordingly.

children.title: Abstract classes hierarchy example
children.summary: Explains how abstract classes affect the generated Cúram Java hierarchy. Using the earlier SubClass/MyClass example, it shows that if MyClass is marked abstract, it cannot be instantiated and its hierarchy includes intf, base, and impl classes, with base realizing the interface and acting as superclass. The section highlights inheritance relationships between generated and handcrafted classes, and how subclasses inherit declarations and implementations from abstract superclasses. Useful for understanding class generation structure and abstract entity modeling.

children.title: Class hierarchy considerations
Public and protected access control
children.summary: Explains Cúram class hierarchy access rules and instantiation constraints. In generated model code, only public and protected are used; private and package access are unnecessary because classes span packages. Handcrafted Java may still use private/public as needed. Clarifies that `super` in handcrafted `<ProjectName>.impl.SubClass` refers to `<ProjectName>.base.SubClass`, not the original superclass. Also stresses that entity, facade, and process objects must be created via their factories, with impl classes made abstract to prevent direct instantiation and preserve class replacement.

children.title: The meaning of super
children.summary: Explains Java superclass references in Cúram class hierarchies: in handcrafted `<ProjectName>.impl.SubClass`, `super` refers to `<ProjectName>.base.SubClass`, not the original base entity superclass. Also notes Cúram model access control uses only public and protected, since generated classes span packages, and emphasizes enforcing factory creation by making implementation classes abstract so objects are instantiated only via factory classes.

children.title: Enforcing the factory mechanism
children.summary: Explains how Cúram enforces object creation through factory classes for entity, facade, and process objects. Direct instantiation with `new` should be avoided; implementation classes in `impl` packages are made abstract to prevent bypassing factories and to ensure class replacement works correctly. The section sits within broader class hierarchy considerations, alongside access control and the meaning of `super`.

Cúram class hierarchy rules cover three points: generated model code uses only public and protected access because classes span packages; handcrafted Java may still use private/public as needed. In handcrafted `<ProjectName>.impl.SubClass`, `super` refers to `<ProjectName>.base.SubClass`, not the original superclass. Entity, facade, and process objects must be created via factory classes, with `impl` classes kept abstract to prevent direct instantiation and preserve class replacement.

children.title: Class hierarchy summary
children.summary: This section explains the generated class hierarchy in Cúram: handcrafted logic belongs in one Java class, public and non-public interfaces are separated, all declared interface methods must be implemented, subclasses can transparently replace superclasses, and factories determine runtime type. It also introduces Cúram JMS queue connectors for qconnector operations, where parameters are converted to JMS messages, sent to queues, optionally awaited for responses, and converted back to structs. It notes when handcrafted customization is needed and outlines the reflection-based encoding process.

Explains Cúram’s generated class hierarchy for model classes. A basic entity maps to four classes: intf (public interface), base (generated abstract implementation with data access/connectors and abstract protected/exit-point methods), impl (handcrafted abstract subclass for custom methods), and fact factory (newInstance() and the only supported instantiation path). Shows how subclasses add inheritance links, including base inheriting the superclass impl, and how abstract classes remove the factory. Also covers access control, the meaning of super in impl classes, and enforcing factory-based creation.

children.title: 1.27 Cúram JMS queue connectors
JMS queue connectors overview
children.summary: Explains Cúram JMS queue connectors for facade and process operations with stereotype qconnector. Generated code converts operation parameters into JMS messages, sends them to a queue, optionally waits for a reply, converts the reply back to a Cúram struct, and returns it. Notes that handcrafted code is usually unnecessary but may be needed for custom datatype encoding or complex parameter structs. Describes use of a connection factory from JNDI, reflection-based field scanning, BytesMessage construction, and reply handling with timeout.

children.title: qconnector operation options
children.summary: This section lists qconnector operation settings: mandatory JNDI names for the QueueConnectionFactory and transmission queue, plus optional reply-queue and response timeout (default 30 seconds) for operations with return values. It explains choosing BytesMessage or TextMessage, and when TextMessage is preferable for character-encoding compatibility. It also covers the BytesMessage encoding character set option and notes it is irrelevant when using TextMessage.

children.title: qconnector operation considerations
Determine the message format or formats and create corresponding struct or structs
children.summary: Agree message formats with the application developer, then create one or more structs to match them. Define the format, length, and ordering of each field; default encodings usually apply, but custom encoding or field mappers can change behavior. Field order in the message must match the struct attribute order. For qconnector operations, the application meta-model method must have one struct parameter, may return void or a struct, and must include options to identify the MQSeries queues.

children.title: Add the operation to the application meta-model
children.summary: Defines how to add a qconnector operation to the application meta-model. It explains that the operation is modeled like a process/facade operation but must follow qconnector restrictions: exactly one struct parameter, optional void or struct return, and options to identify the MQSeries queues and queuing parameters. It also notes that message format, field format/length, and field ordering must be agreed with the application developer, with ordering matching the struct attribute order unless changed.

children.title: Configure the queues in the application server
children.summary: This section explains how to configure queue JNDI names in the application server by mapping them to actual MQSeries/JMS connection factories and queues. It also outlines remote message recipient behavior, including handling optional responses via CorrelationID/MessageID. Finally, it lists qconnector rules and restrictions: valid only in process/facade classes, exactly one struct parameter, return type void or struct, and only flat structs are directly supported; complex structs require a custom mapper.

children.title: Implement the message recipient in the remote system
children.summary: Explains how to implement the remote message recipient for a qconnector: the target system (often legacy) must access MQSeries queues, decode incoming messages, and optionally create and send a response. If a return type is specified, the response must set CorrelationID to the original message’s MessageID. Also notes qconnector constraints: only in process/facade classes, exactly one struct parameter, return type void or struct, and only flat structs are code-generated without custom mappers.

This section explains how to define qconnector operations in the meta-model and prepare matching message structs. It covers agreeing message field formats, lengths, and ordering with the application developer, with struct attribute order controlling message order by default. It also notes that qconnector operations are modeled like process/facade operations but must include exactly one struct parameter, may return void or a struct, and require options to identify MQSeries queues and related queuing parameters.

children.title: qconnector rules and restrictions
children.summary: Qconnector rules and restrictions: qconnector operations are allowed only in process or facade classes, must take exactly one struct parameter, and may return void or a struct. Generated code supports only “flat” structs with fixed-length fields; nested or complex structs require a custom mapper to encode/decode messages. This section also notes that qconnector queue names are stored in JNDI and mapped to application server queues, and that remote systems must send a response with CorrelationID set to the original MessageID when a return type is used.

children.title: Encoding methods for fundamental types
children.summary: Lists how fundamental SVR datatypes are encoded into fixed-width messages. Covers datatype-to-width mappings and formats: booleans as 0/1, char as 1-character string, dates as yyyyMMdd, datetimes as yyyyMMddThhmmss, and numerics as right-justified human-readable strings. Notes that SVR_BLOB and SVR_STRING are padded to model-defined length, while SVR_UNBOUNDED_STRING is not natively supported but can be handled with a custom mapper.

children.title: Customized encoding and decoding class usage
children.summary: Explains how to override default MQSeries connector field encoding/decoding with custom mapper classes. Custom mappers are configured in QueueConnectorFieldMappers.properties on the application classpath using [class].[operation].[param].[field]=[mapper], where the mapper must extend MQFieldMapper. Gives examples for parameter and return fields.
