Also introduces a variable-length field example: prefix data with a six-character length header, with the remote system needing compatible translation.

children.title: Working with variable length fields example
children.summary: Explains how to override default MQ field encoding/decoding in Cúram with custom mapper classes via QueueConnectorFieldMappers.properties. The example shows a variable-length string field in a PersonHistory struct, where historyText is encoded with a 6-character length prefix and decoded by a custom VariableStringMapper extending MQFieldMapper. It highlights that the mapper must be specified separately for both parameter and return fields, and notes the remote system must support the same encoding scheme.

children.title: Working with lists example
children.summary: Shows how to use a custom field mapper to encode/decode a list of structs in a MQSeries connector. PersonDtls is fixed-length (18 chars), and PersonDtlsList is serialized as a padded header with the number of entries followed by concatenated encoded structs. Includes pseudo code for the structs and BPO interface, QueueConnectorFieldMappers.properties entries for both input and return fields, and Java example methods for encode/decode and per-entry struct handling.

Defines Cúram JMS qconnector operations for facade/process classes: the generator converts a single struct parameter to a JMS message, sends it to a queue, and optionally waits for a reply struct. Covers mandatory JNDI queue connection factory/transmission queue settings, optional reply queue and timeout, BytesMessage vs TextMessage, and encoding charset. Explains modeling rules, flat-struct limitations, correlation ID handling, default datatype encodings, and how to override them with MQFieldMapper custom mappers. Includes examples for variable-length fields and list-of-struct encoding.

children.title: 1.28 Subclass modeling
Basic subclassing
children.summary: Explains basic subclassing in Cúram modeling: use subclassing for process, facade, entity, and wsinbound classes to add behavior or override existing behavior, but not to add extra attributes to entities or structs. Examples include adding stereotyped methods, contributing to entity/operation exit points, and changing Readmulti Max options. To create a subclass, add a generalization from subclass to superclass; the subclass inherits all superclass operations and may add functions or modify function options.

children.title: Replacing the superclass
children.summary: Explains how to make a subclass replace its superclass using the Replace_Superclass property, so requests for the base class instantiate the subclass transparently. Also covers abstract classes: setting Abstract=yes prevents instantiation, omits a factory class, and requires impl code unless there are no subclasses. Notes generator limits: no multiple inheritance and subclassing can only add/override operations, not attributes. Includes basic guidance and recommendations for writing subclass code and source-file placement.

children.title: Abstract classes
children.summary: Explains how to mark a class as abstract in the meta-model and what that means in generated Java: no factory class is generated, so the class cannot be instantiated and is intended for subclassing. Abstract classes must have implementation Java code unless they have no subclasses; subclasses must implement any remaining methods. Also notes subclassing limits in Cúram: no multiple inheritance and subclassing can only add or override operations, not attributes, plus brief guidance on where to place new subclass source files.

children.title: Restrictions
children.summary: Cúram subclassing restrictions: multiple inheritance is not supported, and subclassing can only add or override operations, not attributes. The section also notes that there are no special coding restrictions for subclassing entity, facade, or process classes, but recommends placing new subclasses in new source files following the generated hierarchy and in the appropriate EJBServer components source subdirectory. It also covers related behavior for replacing a superclass and for abstract classes, including factory generation and implementation requirements.

children.title: Writing code for subclassing
children.summary: This section explains subclassing behavior in Cúram. It covers replacing a superclass so requests for the base class transparently return the subclass, and making classes abstract so they cannot be instantiated and must be extended. It notes that abstract classes need implementation code, with unimplemented methods supplied by subclasses. It also states that subclassing supports adding or overriding operations only, not attributes, and that there are no special coding restrictions. Recommended practice is to create subclasses in new source files under the generated package structure in the EJBServer components directory.

children.title: Using subclassing to override validation exit points example
children.summary: Explains how to override validation exit points in an entity subclass: enable Automatic validation on the subclass and specify at least one superclass insert or modify operation. Gives an example where a subclass inherits key/details and adds insert/modify operations. Also briefly covers overriding Pre Data Access, Post Data Access, and On-Fail exit points by defining the superclass operations in the subclass and enabling the corresponding options. References related validation and exit point documentation.

children.title: Overriding Pre Data Access, Post Data Access, and On-Fail exit points example
children.summary: Explains how to override Pre Data Access, Post Data Access, and On-fail exit points in an entity subclass. To do so, define the superclass operations in the subclass and then enable the appropriate exit point options on the subclass operations. Example given: EntitySubClass inherits EntityClass and enables On Fail on insert, Post Data Access on read, and Pre Data Access on modify. Briefly notes exit points are callback functions executed at strategic server points.

This section explains Cúram subclass modeling for process, facade, entity, and wsinbound classes. It covers when to use subclassing to add/override operations, not attributes, how inheritance works, and how to replace a superclass transparently with Replace_Superclass. It also describes abstract classes, generator behavior, and restrictions such as no multiple inheritance. Finally, it gives guidance on writing subclass code and examples for overriding validation and other entity exit points.

children.title: 1.29 Application customization
children.summary: Explains how to customize an application without losing changes during upgrades by storing customizations separately from the original model. Highlights three supported approaches: extension classes, overriding domain definitions, and subclass modeling. Notes that extension classes add options without changing the target meta-model, domain definitions can be overridden via SDEJ (especially for third-party definitions), and subclassing can add or override functionality for process, facade, entity, and wsinbound classes.

Reference for the Cúram UML modeling meta-model and generator. Covers packages, stereotypes, data types, and the main class types: domains, entities, extensions, facades, processes, structs, auditing, aggregation, assignable mappings, foreign keys, indexes/unique indexes, JMS qconnectors, handcrafted SQL, subclass modeling, and application customization options. Explains how RSA Designer input is turned by the Cúram Server Code Generator and Data Manager into Java, XML, and database SQL/DDL, plus the generated class hierarchy and modeling rules/restrictions for each construct.
